cases.clinicalHistory: Assess rotator cuff
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 1 - Partial thickness articular sided supraspinatus tear

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Supraspinatus tendinosis: background of some increased signal within the tendon (but not as high as fluid signal) extends to insertion.
  • Low to intermediate grade articular sided tearing of insertional fibres of supraspinatus and anterior fibres of infraspinatus (see sag and scroll from medial to lateral at insertion), approximately 2 x 2 cm. Notice the fluid-like signal within the tendon. Recall that with a tendon tear, the signal is equal to fluid and is more well defined than tendinosis. Notice the bursal surface is intact.
  • Mild joint effusion and synovitis: fluid within the joint, and debris / frond-like thickening within the fluid. Normal joint fluid should not have debris / frond-like thickening.

Other Findings (not the focus of this case):

  • Mild insertional tendinosis of subscapularis, without tearing
  • Sub-cortical cystic changes superior posterior aspect humeral head adjacent to insertional fibres of the inferior portion of the infraspinatus tendon
  • Tearing of superior glenoid labrum with signal extending into biceps tendon. There are features of circumferential tearing of the labrum (scroll)
  • Moderate to severe degenerative changes of AC joint
  • Some chondral loss/thinning along medial superior articular surface of humeral head
  • No focal fatty atrophy or denervation muscle edema.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Let's review a practical approach to the rotator cuff, the main pathology in this case, and cover the important topic of how to assess the muscles.

Practical Approach to Reading the Rotator Cuff

When reading shoulder MRI, start with a systematic approach to the rotator cuff. After orienting yourself, for each tendon, decide whether it's normal, shows tendinopathy, has a partial-thickness tear, or full-thickness tear.

If a tear is present:

  • Identify which surface is involved: articular, bursal, or intrasubstance.
  • Describe the size of the tear in AP and ML dimensions, degree of footprint involvement, and any retraction.

Just as important as evaluating the tendon is assessing the muscles. Muscle quality strongly influences prognosis and surgical repairability, sometimes more than tear size alone.

Partial Articular-Sided Supraspinatus Tears

In this case, the partial articular-sided supraspinatous tear involves the insertional fibres at the footprint on humerus. This subtype of partial thickness tears (involving articular surface at footprint) is referred to as a "PASTA" lesion (Partial Articular Surface Tendon Avulsion) or "rim-rent" tear.

What to know about PASTA lesions:

  • Most common type of partial-thickness rotator cuff tear
  • Frequently under called because they can be subtle and easily mistaken for tendinosis
  • Often symptomatic, may enlarge over time
  • Prognosis and management depend on both the tear characteristics and the underlying muscle quality.

Sequences to use:

  • Best evaluated on coronal oblique images, particularly on PD or T2 fat-saturated sequences, where fluid along articular surface is most conspicuous.
  • Axial images can help assess the footprint and adjacent tendons, but usually secondary.
  • Sagittal oblique images best for muscle bulk, and T1 / non FS sequences to evaluate muscle bulk and fatty infiltration. Always evaluate muscles, even for small tears. The tear can often be identified on ultrasound, but MRI provides the best assessment of muscle quality.

teachingSections.name: Rotator Cuff Muscle: Fatty Infiltration and Atrophy
teachingSections.html:
Fatty Infiltration and Atrophy are related but different. Both should be assessed.

Fatty infiltration is assessed using the Goutallier classification (not 'bulk'):

  • Grade 0 represents normal muscle with no fat
  • Grade 1 shows a few fatty streaks
  • Grade 2 has more muscle than fat
  • Grade 3 shows roughly equal fat and muscle
  • Grade 4 indicates more fat than muscle

In practical terms, people often consider grade 0-1 "normal", 2 moderate, and 3-4 advanced fatty infiltration.

As the Goutallier grade increases, it reflects more chronic disease and is associated with poorer surgical outcomes including higher retear rates. Fatty infiltration is often irreversible, even after successful tendon repair.

A k﻿ey landmark for supraspinatus muscle assessment is the oblique sagittal Y-view - for both fatty infiltration and bulk.

Normally, the supraspinatus muscle bulk should fill the supraspinatus fossa. To be more precise, if you draw a line from upper scapular spine to upper coracoid, normally there should be muscle belly above the line (like in our case). If not ('positive tangent sign'), consider it reduced bulk / atrophic.

Always compare with adjacent muscles and, when available, the contralateral side.

The trapezius muscle (also see coronal) is often a good baseline to use as the patient's background muscle quality. Deltoid muscle can also be used, but can show atrophy or edema with chronic rotator cuff pathology.

This case shows no atrophy or significant fatty infiltration, but we will use these concepts in future cases.

cases.clinicalHistory: Painful arc and weakness of abduction of shoulder
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 2 - Partial bursal sided supraspinatus tendon tear

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Partial thickness bursal sided tear of the supraspinatus tendon. On this image, you can see the intact articular side fibres (arrow). This is high-grade partial (>50% thickness).
  • On sagittal images, you might be mistaken to think of this as a full thickness tear (common pitfall). It is very important to evaluate the tendon in the coronal plane as well, in detail.
  • Subacromial sub deltoid bursal fluid. Presence of fluid within the subacromial bursa is a clue of the presence of a tear that is bursal sided.
  • Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis

A combination of the above three; bursal sided supraspinatus tendon tear, subacromial bursitis, ACJ osteoarthritis, is highly suggestive of subacromial impingement.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a case of a partial-thickness tear involving the bursal surface of the supraspinatus tendon. The articular surface fibers remain intact.

Bursal-sided tears are important because they’re often associated with subacromial impingement, they can be more symptomatic than articular-sided partial tears, and their surgical management may differ.

Imaging Planes:

  • Coronal oblique images are the most useful plane for evaluating the bursal surface of supraspinatus, particularly on PD or T2 fat-saturated sequences.
  • Sagittal oblique images help assess the overall extent of the tear and muscle quality.

Associated Findings to Look For:

  • Supraspinatus tendinosis frequently present
  • Acromial morphology or a subacromial spur may contribute to impingement and help explain the tear pattern.
  • Cystic change in the greater tuberosity can be seen, and the long head of the biceps tendon should always be evaluated for concurrent pathology.

Pearls & Pitfalls:

  • Severe bursal-sided tendinosis can mimic a partial tear, so focal surface fluid and disruption are more convincing than signal change alone.
  • Small surface defects can be overlooked if there is little associated bursitis.
  • Magic angle artifact can also increase signal in the supraspinatus tendon, so it’s important to confirm the abnormality across multiple planes, sequences, and contiguous slices.

A useful tip is that bursal-sided tears usually show adjacent bursitis, and that surface irregularity combined with focal fluid is more specific for a true tear than tendon signal abnormality alone. Always assess the acromial morphology and subacromial space.

teachingSections.name: Intro to "Impingement"
teachingSections.html:
This comes up a lot. You need to know what it means and the types.

Impingement refers to rotator cuff / bursa being "pinched" during arm movement. We cover the broader types below, but the two main ones to know are subacromial (external) and posterosuperior (internal).


External Impingement:

  • Refers broadly to pinching from the 'outside' of joint (hence external) -- bursal sided compression
  • ***Most common site: Between humeral head and coracoacromial arch (acromion, coracoacromial ligament, coracoid). This is what most people mean when say 'impingement' colloquially (classic subacromial impingement).
  • Causes: Hooked Acromion (type III), subacromial/AC joint osteophytes, thickened coracoacromial ligament
  • Less Common: "Subcoracoid impingement" can also occur between coracoid and humerus (affecting subscapularis tendon) if narrowed. We discuss this in a later case.

Internal Impingement (intra-articular)

  • Refers broadly to pinching on undersurface of rotator cuff (from the "internal" / joint side) -- where the cuff contacts glenoid (therefore articular surface).
  • ***Most Commonly this occurs at Posterosuperior shoulder -- In throwing position / 'ABER' (abduction, external rotation). Pinches undersurface of supraspinatus/anterior infraspinatus at posterosuperior glenoid (often partial articular surface tears)
  • Less common: Anterosuperior - contact between subscap / biceps pulley and anterosuperior glenoid/labrum

cases.clinicalHistory: Shoulder pain and decreasing mobility
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 3 - Full thickness supraspinatus tendon tear

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Complete (full-thickness full-width) supraspinatus tendon tear.
  • See sagittal, with no tendon fibres visible at attachment. There is no visible tendon fibre between the articular surface and the subacromial bursa, with fluid communicating between articular space and the bursa.
  • Medial retraction of the supraspinatous tendon, approximately 3.2 cm.
  • Mild supraspinatus muscle volume loss. The supraspinatus muscle belly has lost volume and does not fill the supraspinous fossa completely (please review case 1 muscle bulk discussion)

Other Findings of note (not the reason for showing this case):

  • Biceps tendon situated normally in bicipital groove, with mild tendinopathy (increased signal without tear) just superior to this as it extends into the groove
  • Severe AC joint OA (here, here)
  • Sublabral recess (normal variant) without significant labral pathology on this routine study (we cover labrum in more detail in later cases)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case shows a full thickness supraspinatus tendon tear. By definition, this is a disruption of the supraspinatus tendon from the articular surface all the way through to the bursal surface, creating direct communication between the glenohumeral joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Look for a fluid-signal cleft traversing the entire thickness of the tendon, from the articular to the bursal surface. Subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid is typically present and supports the diagnosis.

The coronal oblique images are usually the best place to identify a full-thickness tear, as they most clearly demonstrate tendon discontinuity at the greater tuberosity footprint. Fluid sensitive sequences highlight fluid signal within the tear.

Sagittal oblique images are essential for assessing muscle bulk (reduced here), and bulk and fatty infiltration and should always be reviewed alongside the tendon abnormality (as mentioned in prior case).


There are several associated findings that help you assess the severity and chronicity of a full-thickness tear:

  • Muscle atrophy and fatty infiltration of the supraspinatus are common and have major prognostic implications.
  • You may see cystic change at the greater tuberosity, pathology of the long head of the biceps tendon, and prominent subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis.
  • In long-standing (or large) tears, you see more severe narrowing of the acromiohumeral interval (shortest distance from inferior acromion to superior humeral head), with superior migration of the humeral head (high riding humeral head).


Pitfalls worth keeping in mind:

  • Severe tendinosis can sometimes mimic a tear, so the presence of a fluid-filled cleft extending from the joint to the bursa is key.
  • Tear size can be underestimated if scarring or adhesions partially obscure the defect, so it’s important to follow the tendon across multiple slices.
  • It’s easy to focus on the tendon defect itself and overlook muscle quality, which often has greater prognostic value than tear size alone.

To help me identify the margins of the supraspinatus (or infraspinatus) tendons, I start medially and then follow the anterior and posterior margins of the tendon from the level of the muscle itself to the expected insertion on humeral head.

cases.clinicalHistory: Pain and reduced range of motion anterior shoulder, difficulty staying still

series.label: COR OBL T2 FSE FATSAT RT

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Technically suboptimal study due to motion.

Subscapularis tendon partial thickness tear (also on sagittal - scroll) with associated atrophy/fatty infiltration of muscle (also see Sag T1):

  • Notice thinning of subscapularis with a fluid cleft deep to the superior fibres of the subscapularis tendon, consistent with a tear. Feathery appearance of the subscapularis muscle, consistent with chronic tear leading to atrophy and fatty infiltration of the muscle.

Long head biceps tendon is located within the bicipital groove. But, note longitudinal split tear of long head of biceps tendon (there are two discrete tendon components of the long head of biceps tendon, in keeping with a longitudinal split).

Additional Rotator Cuff Tears:

  • Full thickness tear of anterior supraspinatus tendon (also on coronal). Notice the fluid cleft replacing the anterior fibres of the supraspinatus tendon. Reduced bulk of supraspinatous muscle
  • Low grade partial thickness articular sided tearing of the infraspinatus on a background of tendinosis. Mild infraspinatus muscle atrophy.

Other Findings:

  • AC joint OA. Articular narrowing and irregularity of the bony margins of clavicle and acromion at the AC joint.
  • Moderate degenerative changes at glenohumeral joint
  • Labrum poorly assessed

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
When evaluating the rotator cuff, subscapularis tears deserve special attention because they’re commonly missed / under-reported. This is because:

  • They don’t show up as well on the coronal images we rely on for supraspinatus.
  • They are often tied to biceps tendon and pulley pathology -- so of you're not considering that relationship the diagnosis can slip by (no pun intended).

Clinically, these tears matter because they contribute to anterior shoulder pain and instability, and because associated biceps pathology often changes management.

Key Anatomy:

  • Unlike other rotator cuff tendons with a single single tendinous bundle, subscapularis has 3-5 discrete tendon bundles that insert to the lesser tuberosity (multipennate). The upper part is tendinous, while the lower third is mostly muscular and spared until late. This image shows the normal anatomy (arrowheads superior and asterisks inferior portion).

Imaging Planes:

  • Axial images are the most sensitive plane for evaluating the subscapularis tendon, particularly on PD or T2 fat-saturated sequences, which best show tendon disruption and fluid signal.
  • Coronal images alone can significantly underestimate the presence and extent of a subscapularis tear, so you should never clear the subscapularis without carefully scrolling through the Ax and Sag.

Subscapularis Tendon Tears:

  • The most typical tear pattern involves the upper fibers of the subscapularis and is often partial thickness. These upper-fiber tears are the ones most closely associated with biceps instability (see anatomy below). Full-thickness subscapularis tears tend to occur in more advanced or chronic disease.
  • On MRI, key findings include thinning or discontinuity of the subscapularis tendon at its insertion on the lesser tuberosity, as well as fluid signal within or replacing the tendon fibers.
  • One of the most important associated findings is medial subluxation or dislocation of the long head of the biceps tendon.

There are several secondary findings that can help you, especially in more chronic tears. These include subscapularis muscle atrophy or fatty infiltration and cystic change at the lesser tuberosity. As with the other cuff tendons, muscle quality has important prognostic implications and should always be assessed on sagittal oblique T1 images.

Important Pearl:

If the long head of the biceps tendon is medially subluxed or dislocated, you should assume there is a subscapularis tear until proven otherwise. Upper subscapularis tears are best appreciated by scrolling through multiple sequential axial slices rather than relying on a single image. Tendinosis without actual fiber disruption should not cause biceps instability, so if the biceps is abnormal, look harder for a tear.


Introducing the Biceps Pulley:

We will cover this in greater detail in future cases. But:

The biceps pulley in the rotator interval is the key stabilizer of long head of biceps (LHB) as it turns into the groove. People make this more complex than it needs to be for real reporting. The key contributors are: superior glenohumeral ligament (SGHL) and coracohumeral ligament (CHL), with some contributors from upper subscapularis and supraspinatous fibres.

The so-called 'transverse humeral ligament' (THL) sits over the biciptal groove more inferiorly, largely composed of superficial subscap fibres that cross over to insert on greater tuberosity.

cases.clinicalHistory: Pain and weakness posterior shoulder
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 5 - Delaminating intrasubstance tear of infraspinatus tendon

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Horizontal split with intrasubstance delamination and retraction of infeaspinatus tendon, with musculotendinous disruption. The arrow shows the retracted tip of the torn infraspinatus tendon.
  • The tendon has delaminated from the adjacent fibres and has retracted medially along the muscle.

The tendon tear site is nicely shown by following tendon on Sag. Starting laterally and scrolling medially:

  • You see tendon laterally
  • Following medially, now you don't see tendon (scroll)
  • As you keep following medially, and now you see where the tendon is retracted to
  • Associated intramuscular edema

Other Findings:

  • Supraspinatus tendon intact
  • Teres minor and subscapularis tendons are unremarkable

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is an uncommon and easily mis-diagnosed tear: the intrasubstance, or delaminating, tear of the infraspinatus tendon. This is a horizontal tear within the substance of the tendon, where the tendon layers separate from each other while both the articular and bursal surfaces remain intact. This is also referred to as a "Novel Lesion" of infraspinatus.

They can cause significant shoulder pain even though the rotator cuff footprint appears intact. They’re often missed on routine MRI reads and even at arthroscopy if the surgeon isn’t specifically looking for them. In some cases, different layers of the tendon may retract to different degrees, which adds to the diagnostic challenge.


Coronal oblique and axial images are the most helpful planes for identifying these tears, and PD or T2 fat-saturated sequences are key because they highlight intratendinous fluid.


On MRI, a delaminating infraspinatus tear appears as a linear or cleft-like focus of fluid signal within the substance of the tendon. The signal typically runs parallel to the tendon fibers rather than perpendicular to them. The tendon insertion on the greater tuberosity remains intact, which is why these lesions are so easy to miss if you’re only looking for footprint disruption. You may also notice focal tendon thickening or subtle contour irregularity at the site of delamination.

There are several associated findings that can support the diagnosis. You may see edema at the infraspinatus myotendinous junction or within the muscle itself, adjacent tendinosis, or subacromial–subdeltoid bursitis. In early cases, muscle atrophy and fatty infiltration are minimal or absent, which can falsely reassure you if you’re only thinking in terms of chronic full-thickness tears.

cases.clinicalHistory: Pain and reduced range of motion

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Complete tear of supraspinatus tendon, with retraction to level of acromioclavicular joint (bald headed greater tuberosity). Decreased bulk of supraspinatus muscle

Identify the footprint of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons at the greater tuberotisty which is bare, and has a peak, which can be useful to delineate the junction between the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.

  • Complete tear of infraspinatus tendon, with retraction (often the supraspintaus and infraspinatus tendons can scar with each other, scroll from here up to here). Again absent tendon at the footprint, with retraction medially.
  • Partial thickness tear of the subscapularis tendon (Often the most inferior, muscular portion of subscapualris insertion remains intact)
  • Medial subluxation of long head of biceps tendon (always look for a subscapularis tendon tear when you see this). Tearing of long head of biceps just above the groove.
  • High riding humeral head with reduced acromiohumeral interval (This always suggests a chronic tear as acute tears don’t cause high riding of the humeral head). This is associated with cartilage thinning along the humeral head.

Other findings:

  • Moderate fatty infiltration of supraspinatus and infraspinatus
  • Fraying of the superior labrum. The labrum appears blunted and has lost its usual triangular shape.
  • Joint effusion with reactive synovitis. There is excess fluid within the joint, and the fluid is not clean and has debris within it (window)
  • Mild AC joint OA

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A massive rotator cuff tear refers to a full-thickness tear involving two or more rotator cuff tendons, OR a tear measuring more than five centimeters in greatest dimension.

These tears matter because they’re often irreparable or technically very challenging to repair. They’re strongly associated with marked muscle atrophy, advanced fatty infiltration, and poor functional outcomes. Identifying a tear as massive early in your interpretation helps set expectations and shifts your focus toward chronicity and global cuff function rather than just measuring the defect.

Things to know:

Most commonly involved tendons are the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, and in more advanced cases the tear may extend anteriorly to involve the subscapularis as well. On MRI, you’ll typically see full-thickness tendon discontinuity with a large, fluid-filled gap and loss of normal rotator cuff footprint coverage. There is usually clear communication between the glenohumeral joint and the subacromial–subdeltoid bursa.

Tendon retraction is a key feature and often extends medially toward the humeral head or all the way to the glenoid, depending on chronicity. Coronal oblique images are useful for appreciating the extent of the tear, while axial images help confirm multi-tendon involvement. Often, chronic massive tears with retraction of the supaspinatus and infraspinatus tendons can lead to these tendons scarring to each other, as shown in this case.

Marked muscle atrophy and advanced fatty infiltration are common and should be assessed carefully on sagittal oblique T1 images (if available) to understand the global status of the rotator cuff.

Superior migration of the humeral head is frequently present and results in a reduced acromiohumeral interval, which strongly suggests a chronic tear. Pathology of the long head of the biceps tendon is also commonly associated, as shown in this case.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid with evidence of synovitis. Notice there is fluid filling the bursa, with areas of debris and irregularity within the fluid.
  • Supraspinatus tendinosis (long arrow) with associated partial thickness tearing (diffuse high-grade partial articular and bursal, with focal full-thickness perforation). Thickened, heterogeneous appearance of the tendon with fluid clefts.
  • Subscapularis tendinosis. Thickening and heterogeneity of the tendon. Long head of biceps tendinosis
  • Joint effusion with synovitis

Other findings:

  • Fat containing intramuscular lesion within the short head of biceps muscle. This most likely represents a lipoma, but is incompletely covered in the field of view, and will need dedicated imaging.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Normally, the subacromial–subdeltoid bursa is a potential space and should contain little to no visible fluid. When it becomes distended and inflamed, it can be a significant pain generator and is often a clue to underlying shoulder pathology rather than a primary diagnosis by itself.

Recognizing bursitis and putting it in the proper clinical context is important, because it frequently reflects rotator cuff disease, impingement, or other adjacent abnormalities that drive management.


The coronal oblique images are the best place to assess the extent of the subacromial–subdeltoid bursa, particularly on PD or T2 fat-saturated sequences, where fluid and synovial inflammation are most conspicuous. Axial images are helpful for determining how far the bursal fluid extends anteriorly and posteriorly and for confirming lateral extension beneath the deltoid muscle.

On MRI, bursitis appears as fluid distension of the subacromial–subdeltoid bursa. You may also see synovial thickening, internal debris, or septations within the bursal fluid. In more pronounced cases, fluid tracks laterally beneath the deltoid. If post-contrast images are available, enhancement of the thickened bursal lining can be seen and supports active inflammation.

Subacromial–subdeltoid bursitis is most commonly associated with rotator cuff tendinosis or tears and with subacromial impingement. It can also be seen in calcific tendinitis, as well as in post-traumatic or postoperative settings. Because of these associations, bursitis should almost always prompt a careful search for underlying cuff pathology.

Isolated bursitis without an obvious cuff tear should make you look more closely for subtle tendon pathology or consider inflammatory or crystal-related disease. The degree of synovial thickening is often more clinically relevant than the absolute volume of fluid alone. And when possible, comparing the appearance of the bursa with the contralateral side can help you judge whether mild fluid is truly abnormal.

A triad of supraspinatus tendinosis/tear, subacromial bursitis and ACJ osteoarthritis should raise the concern for subacromial impingement.

cases.clinicalHistory: Pain and reduced range of motion, with reduced flexion strength of upper arm
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 8 - Long head of biceps tendon dislocation

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Medial dislocation of the long head of biceps tendon (follow the course of the tendon from here to here). The tendon is located medial to the bicipital groove (where it should be located normally).
  • Partial thickness tear of subscapularis tendon with biceps entering into the tear

Other Findings:

  • Partial thickness articular sided tear of supraspinatus tendon. There is attenuation of the substance of the supraspinatus tendon at its footprint with reduced thickness of the tendon substance. It can be difficult to accurately characterize the articular or bursal sided extension of this tear.
  • Severe acromioclavicular joint OA. There is articular surface irregularity with reactive marrow edema involving the distal clavicle and the acromion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
When you’re reading a shoulder MRI, medial dislocation of the long head of the biceps tendon is a critical finding that should immediately get your attention. This finding indicates a high-grade subscapularis tendon tear and represents failure of the biceps pulley.

Axial images are the most diagnostic plane for evaluating the position of the long head of the biceps tendon. Sagittal images are helpful for assessing chronicity, muscle atrophy, and fatty infiltration, especially in the subscapularis.

On MRI, the key finding is absence of the long head of the biceps tendon from the bicipital groove (a classic exam question). Instead, the tendon is displaced medially and can be seen within or along the subscapularis tendon. You may also see disruption or abnormality of the biceps pulley, along with thinning or discontinuity of the subscapularis tendon itself.


Medial biceps dislocation is accompanied by:

  • Subscapularis tendon tear, typically involving the upper fibers
  • Associated long head of the biceps tendinosis or tearing is common
  • Additional rotator cuff pathology, especially involving the supraspinatus, is frequently present.
  • Subacromial–subdeltoid bursitis may also be seen as a secondary finding.

Key takeaway here: A true medial dislocation of the biceps virtually always implies a subscapularis tear. If the biceps is not in the groove, you should actively search for it. Confirm the abnormal biceps position by comparing with the expected location of the groove on adjacent axial slices to avoid misinterpretation.


Biceps Pulley Anatomy:

The biceps pulley in the rotator interval is the key stabilizer of long head of biceps (LHB) as it turns into the groove. What you need to know is less complicated than most diagrams make it out to be. It is best depicted on Sagittal Images, made up mostly of the first two below:

  • Coracohumeral ligament (CHL) - the roof of the rotator interval
  • Superior glenohumeral ligament (SGHL) - a focal thickening of the capsule that rotates around but in the interval sits along inferior margin
  • Blended contributions from with upper subscapularis (which sits inferior to interval) and supraspinatous fibres (which sits superior to the interval.

This is a great image demonstrating the anatomy. The white arrowhead is the LHB in the interval, the white arrow represents CHL as the roof of the pulley, and the black arrow as the SGHL -- forming a sling around the LHB.

Also look at these structures on our normal anatomy case. It will NOT be visible in a not insignificant proportion of patients (about 10%), and so practically speaking, use the position / appearance of LHB tendon as as surrogate for presence of a biceps pulley lesion.

This video explains it really well and shows a few examples, if interested.


The Biceps pulley is distinct from the so-called 'transverse humeral ligament' (THL), which sits over the bicipital groove more inferiorly, largely composed of superficial subscapularis fibres that cross over to insert on greater tuberosity.

cases.clinicalHistory: Patient with anterior shoulder pain

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Reduced space between anterior humeral head and inferior coracoid (reduced coracohumeral distance) - the selected slice is just inferior to this, showing reduced space between humeral head and tendons of short head of biceps and coracobrachialis
  • Subscapularis tendon tear (high grade articular sided). The subscapularis tendon appears thinned at the level of the coracohumeral interval (scroll from medial to lateral)
  • Long head of biceps tear. Very thinned appearance of the long head of biceps tendon, mildly subluxed
  • Glenohumeral joint effusion with synovitis. See fluid in superior subscapularis recess (SSR) with synovitis
  • Mild thickening of the subacromial bursa. This can represent subacromial bursitis in the correct clinical setting.
  • Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Subcoracoid impingement refers to narrowing of the coracohumeral interval, which leads to compression of the subscapularis tendon and the long head of the biceps tendon between the coracoid process and the lesser tuberosity.

This entity matters because it’s an under-recognized cause of anterior shoulder pain and is often associated with subscapularis tendon tears and biceps pathology.

Axial images are the most useful plane for assessing the coracohumeral interval and should be reviewed carefully at the level of the coracoid tip. Sagittal oblique images can help confirm involvement of the anterior rotator cuff and assess muscle quality.

On MRI:

  • Key finding is reduced coracohumeral distance, typically less than six millimeters. Often, pathological narrowing can also be due to subscapularis tendinosis, which effectively reduces the coracohumeral distance, even though, the measurement between the bones may be more than 6 millimeters. as in this case.
  • Narrowing is often accompanied by subscapularis tendinosis or tearing, as well as pathology of the long head of the biceps tendon, including tendinosis, subluxation, or dislocation.
  • You may also see edema or bursitis in the anterior rotator interval, which supports the diagnosis.

Additional findings can include cystic change at the lesser tuberosity, subcoracoid bursitis when present, and anterior shoulder synovitis.

Subcoracoid impingement should be considered in patients with isolated or predominant subscapularis pathology. Always measure the coracohumeral interval on axial images at the level of the coracoid tip rather than estimating it visually. Biceps instability strengthens the diagnosis, as the biceps and subscapularis are commonly affected together. A relevant clinical history of anterior shoulder pain and pain on provocative tests are helpful in making a diagnosis.

cases.clinicalHistory: Post rotator cuff repair with weakness and pain
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 10 - Post operative rotator cuff repair with re-tear

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Evidence of previous rotator cuff repair with suture anchors in the greater tuberosity (used to compress tendon to footprint until healing)
  • Complete tear of supraspinatus tendon with retraction to level of glenohumeral joint. The arrow points to the tip of the torn tendon. Severe supraspinatus muscle belly volume loss
  • Complete tear of infraspinatus tendon with retraction. Again, the arrow points to the tendon tip. Some fibres do make it to greater tuberosity.
  • Absent biceps in groove with retracted tendon distally (this could be due to biceps tenotomy - i.e. biceps tendon released from its origin)
  • Partial articular sided tear of subscapularis tendon, high grade.

Overall, massive rotator cuff tear with failed repair

Other Findings of Note:

  • Humeral head is high riding
  • Extensive degenerative tearing of glenoid labrum
  • Moderate joint effusion with synovitis, extending into / communicating with subacromial-subdeltoid bursa
  • Moderate AC joint OA

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
When you’re reading a postoperative shoulder MRI, one of the most important — and most challenging — questions is whether there is a re-tear of the rotator cuff. Re-tears are a common cause of persistent or recurrent pain after surgery and have a major impact on prognosis and revision surgery planning. At the same time, postoperative tendons almost never look normal, so this diagnosis should be made carefully.

Coronal oblique images are the primary plane for assessing tendon continuity and footprint integrity, while axial images help define the extent of a suspected tear and evaluate associated pathology. In equivocal cases, MR arthrography can be helpful by better outlining fluid tracking through a defect.

The most reliable MRI sign of a new tear is a fluid-signal cleft traversing the tendon. You may see full- or partial-thickness tendon discontinuity, often with tendon retraction at or medial to the repair site. Communication between the glenohumeral joint and the subacromial–subdeltoid bursa further supports the diagnosis of a full-thickness retear. Evaluating the tendon medial to the footprint is especially important, as this is a common site of failure.

Normal Post-op Appearance:

It’s useful to understand what normal postoperative changes look like. A repaired tendon is typically thickened, heterogeneous, and irregular in contour. Increased signal on fluid-sensitive sequences can persist for a long time after surgery. Importantly, a repaired tendon does not return to a normal pre-injury appearance, and heterogeneity alone should not be mistaken for a re-tear. You should look for tendon discontinuity, fluid signal gap, retraction, and communication with bursa to increase confidence of true re-tear.

Associated findings on postoperative MRI commonly include suture anchors with susceptibility artifact, subacromial–subdeltoid bursitis, muscle atrophy and fatty infiltration, and pathology of the long head of the biceps tendon.

Always compare with prior imaging when available, and review the operative report if possible, as it often clarifies what you’re seeing.

Special attention should be paid to the long head of the biceps tendon. On postoperative MRI, an absent intra-articular biceps tendon is often intentional and should not be misinterpreted as a rupture:

  • After a biceps TENOTOMY (released from its origin), the expected finding is absence of the intra-articular biceps tendon (as in this case).
  • After a biceps TENODESIS (tendon detached and then re-fixed), the tendon is fixed to the humerus, usually outside the bicipital groove, and you may see hardware or cortical changes at the fixation site.

Always correlate with operative history and look for fixation-related findings before labeling biceps pathology.

cases.clinicalHistory: Recurrent shoulder instability

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Displaced tear of the anteroinferior glenoid labrum. You can see a clear gap between the labrum and the underlying glenoid with contrast filling that space (this is an arthrogram). Notice that the labrum here is completely detached from glenoid (and periosteum). There is no bony fragment attached. This is a soft tissue Bankart lesion.
  • In addition, this tear extends to involve the superior glenoid labrum (see how the contrast cleft extends behind the biceps anchor), posterior labrum (you can see an increased signal cleft between the posterior labrum and underlying glenoid).
  • Corresponding Hill Sachs lesion - focal wedge / flattened impaction defect of posterosuperior humeral head, due to prior dislocation. Normally, the humeral head should be round on the top axial slices. Here, there is abnormality in the top 4-5 mm (at / above the coracoid). More inferior, there is a normal longitudinal groove that should not be mistaken for a Hill-Sachs.

Overall, there is evidence of prior anteroinferior glenohumeral dislocation, with a Hill-Sachs lesion, soft tissue Bankart with extension into a circumferential labral tear.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Evaluation of the glenoid labrum can be challenging, and there are a lot of named labral abnormalities. In this case, we will discuss evaluation of the labrum, normal variants, and introduce anteroinferior labral abnormalities with a focus on soft tissue Bankart (this case).

Assessing the Labrum

If the clinical question is to assess for a labral tear in a young patient, an MRI arthrogram should be considered (like this case). The expansion of the joint capsule with diluted gadolinium helps separate the capsule away from the labrum and humeral head and facilitates visualization of labral tears as tear clefts filled with contrast.

Evaluate the labrum in multiple planes.

  • Coronal images are useful for assessment of the superior and inferior labrum
  • Axial images visualize anterior and posterior labrum well

Normal Appearance and Anatomic Variants:

Before we discuss labral pathology, it is important to learn the normal appearance and three important normal anatomical variants. Recall the glenoid clockface with 3 o'clock anteriorly.

The labrum is a fibrocartilaginous, low signal structure attached to the glenoid rim -- though with degeneration can get some 'graying'. It is normally triangular.

Anatomic variants mostly occur in the anterosuperior part of the labrum (actually about 10 - 3 o'clock). Know these common ones:

  • Sublabral recess (sulcus) is the most common -- a thin, smooth and narrow incomplete cleft between the labrum and the glenoid. It runs parallel to the glenoid and therefore extends medially, typically 11-2 o'clock position at biceps-labral complex.
  • Sublabral foramen is similar, but less frequent and involves complete gap between labrum and glenoid, usually anterosuperior (12 - 3 o'clock). Importantly, the labrum at this site is away from the glenoid margin, but rejoins at 3 o'clock. This can coexist with a sublabral recess.
  • Buford complex is less frequent, and involves absence of anterosuperior labrum, and a thick cord-like MGHL (middle glenohumeral ligament).

While these can look like tears, the location and appearance are the giveaway. If a “cleft” is only seen at the anterosuperior aspect of the labrum, always consider normal variants described above first. Do not diagnose a labral tear that is limited to the anterosuperior labrum unless certain. This article provides a deeper dive with nice images if interested.

Soft Tissue Bankart

A soft tissue Bankart tear refers to a detachment of the anteroinferior labrum from the glenoid rim without an associated bony fragment. It typically occurs after anterior shoulder dislocation and is a major contributor to recurrent instability.

The anterior shoulder dislocation happens in an anteroinferior direction. Hence, the anteroinferior labrum is the affected location.

Imaging Planes:

  • Axial images are the most important plane for evaluating the anteroinferior labrum and should be reviewed carefully, especially on fluid-sensitive sequences (and arthrograms of course).
  • Coronal oblique images are useful for assessing the inferior extent of the tear and for evaluating associated capsular injury.
  • Sagittal images can help confirm labral displacement and provide a global view of the capsulolabral complex.

Appearance:

On MRI, a soft tissue Bankart tear appears as detachment of the anteroinferior labrum from the glenoid rim, often with interposed fluid. The labrum may appear displaced medially or inferiorly, and the normal triangular labral morphology is lost. There may be associated thickening or redundancy of the anterior capsule, reflecting capsular stripping or injury.

Associated Findings:

Several associated findings often accompany a Bankart tear. These include:

  • Hill–Sachs lesion of the posterolateral humeral head. The imaging appearance is described in the key findings section -- a wedge defect / flattening of posterosuperior humeral head at/above level of coracoid (normally, the top of humeral head should be round). This image reminds you why these occur together.
  • Capsular injury or redundancy
  • Joint effusion
  • In chronic instability, you may see labral scarring or medialization rather than a clean fluid-filled detachment.
  • Subtle bone loss of the anteroinferior glenoid may also be present, even when the lesion is primarily soft tissue.

teachingSections.name: Key Intro: Anteroinferior Labral Tears
teachingSections.html:
There are a bunch of labral pathologies with acronyms in the shoulder that make things confusing. Grouping them helps -- into anteroinferior, posteroinferior, and superior. Here, we introduce the anteroinferior labral abnormalities (3-6 o'clock) -- which occur due to anterior instability, and consist of Bankart lesions and variants. Thus, these are associated with Hill-Sachs lesions.

These are complicated at first, but actually quite simple once you understand what exactly separates them (read key descriptors below and see linked images):

  • Bankart Lesion - detachment of the anteroinferior labrum from glenoid [and from underlying periosteum = completely detached]. This is what we see in our case. If anteroinferior glenoid bone also detaches from glenoid, then it is considered a Bony Bankart. The key here is complete detachment (unlike others below, which all remain attached in some way).
  • ALPSA (Anterior Labroligamentous Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) - detachment of anteroinferior labrum, but labrum remains attached to periosteum. The detached labrum rotates medially to the anterior scapular neck.
  • Perthes Lesion - less common - labrum detaches from glenoid but remains attached to periosteum, AND is not displaced (only minimal stripping). These may be occult on MR, and can spontaneously heal. Think of them as ALPSAs but without medial displacement.
  • GLAD (GlenoLabral Articular Disruption) - rare - non-displaced tear of anteroinferior labrum with associated injury to adjacent cartilage.

We will cover each in more detail in this section, along with posteroinferior and superior labral tears. But, it's worth taking the time to understand the names and descriptions above alongside linked images.

cases.clinicalHistory: Recurrent anterior dislocations

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Truncation of the anterior glenoid, which looks different than the posterior glenoid (usually the anterior and posterior glenoid rims should be mirror images of each other). This extends from 2 to 5 o'clock, involving about 15% of total glenoid surface.
  • The bony Bankart component can sometimes be difficult to identify on MRI (also see sagittal and here).
  • Associated displaced labral tear at the anterior inferior labrum. This extends back to about 10 o'clock (and down to about 6 o'clock). Notice the superior component does not follow the contour of the glenoid (this is not a sublabral recess)
  • Paralabral cyst adjacent to the anteroinferior labrum. Paralabral cysts are often only visible on the T2 weighted images in an arthrogram series.
  • No Hill Sachs in this patient

IMPRESSION:

Bony Bankart with a glenoid labral tear as described.

Subsequent plain film also showed irregularity at anteroinferior glenoid, consistent with a bony Bankart:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
When reviewing a shoulder MRI for instability, a bony Bankart lesion represents a fracture or avulsion of the anteroinferior glenoid rim, with the labrum attached to the bony fragment. This occurs after anterior shoulder dislocation and is a critical finding because the amount of glenoid bone loss strongly predicts recurrent instability and influences whether soft-tissue repair alone will fail.

Identifying and estimating the extent of bone loss is essential for surgical planning, as larger defects may require bony augmentation procedures rather than standard labral repair.

A search for a possible bony Bankart lesion should follow the evaluation of the labrum that we introduced in the previous case:

  • Axial images are the most important plane for detecting anteroinferior glenoid rim fractures and should be carefully reviewed from superior to inferior. Fluid sensitive sequences highlight marrow edema and adjacent fluid, while T1-weighted images help define the cortical margins and chronicity of the fragment.
  • Sagittal images provide an overview of glenoid morphology.
  • Coronal oblique images help assess inferior extension and displacement

MRI Appearance:

  • Bony Bankart appears as a defect or fracture of the anteroinferior glenoid rim, often with a detached osseous fragment.
  • The fragment may be displaced medially or inferiorly and is typically associated with labral detachment.
  • Acute lesions show marrow edema within the fragment and adjacent glenoid, whereas chronic lesions may appear sclerotic, blunted, or remodeled, sometimes with an absent fragment representing attritional bone loss.

Similar to the soft tissue Bankart injury, always look for associated pathology like:

  • Hill Sachs lesion
  • Additional labral tear, or capsular injury
  • In chronic cases, there may be significant anteroinferior glenoid bone loss without a discrete fragment, which functionally behaves like a bony Bankart lesion.

Pitfalls include mistaking normal glenoid contour variants or partial volume averaging for a fracture, especially on a single slice. Small fragments can be missed if axial images are not carefully scrolled, and chronic bone loss may be underrecognized if you focus only on acute fracture signs such as edema.


CT is superior for precise quantification of glenoid bone loss, but MRI can and should raise concern when a bony Bankart lesion is present, like in this case.

cases.clinicalHistory: Recurrent dislocations and chronic pain.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Anteroinferior labral abnormality, with thickened labral tissue displaced medially anterior to neck. Labral tissue looks rolled on itself, and there is an empty anterior rim of glenoid where the anteroinferior labral tissue should normally be.
  • The labral fragment remains attached to periosteum / is not completely separated.
  • Hill Sachs lesion

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
An ALPSA lesion (Anterior Labroligamentous Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) is a variant of an anteroinferior labral injury that occurs after anterior shoulder dislocation. In this lesion, the anteroinferior labrum is torn and displaced medially and inferiorly, but the periosteum remains intact. The labroligamentous complex sits in a non-anatomic position, and can heal in this position.

On MRI, an ALPSA lesion appears as detachment of the anteroinferior labrum with medial displacement along the glenoid neck, rather than at the glenoid rim where it normally resides. The labrum often appears "rolled" up or thickened (rolled up like a sleeve, hence the name), lying against the medial glenoid neck, and the normal triangular labral contour at the rim is absent. Because the periosteum is intact, there may not be any fluid interposed at the rim, making these lesions sometimes more subtle than classic Bankart tears.

MR arthrography can be especially helpful in ALPSA lesions, as intra-articular contrast may outline the displaced labrum and better demonstrate the abnormal medial position of the capsulolabral complex.

Acute Vs Chronic:

  • Acute ALPSA lesion
  • Chronic ALPSA - heals in the abnormal location, resulting in proliferation / deposition of fibrous tissue (like in our case)

teachingSections.html:
We introduced anteroinferior labral tears in previous cases. While complicated at first, they are simpler once you understand them and see examples. Review the below descriptions, images, and cases:

  • Bankart Lesion - detachment of the anteroinferior labrum from glenoid [and from underlying periosteum = completely detached]. If anteroinferior glenoid bone also detaches from glenoid, then it is considered a Bony Bankart. The key here is complete detachment (unlike others below, which all remain attached in some way).
  • **ALPSA (Anterior Labroligamentous Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) - detachment of anteroinferior labrum, but labrum remains attached to periosteum. The detached labrum rotates medially to the anterior scapular neck.
  • Perthes Lesion - less common - labrum detaches from glenoid but remains attached to periosteum, AND is not displaced (only minimal stripping). These may be occult on MR, and can spontaneously heal. Think of them as ALPSAs but without medial displacement.
  • GLAD (GlenoLabral Articular Disruption) - rare - non-displaced tear of anteroinferior labrum with associated injury to adjacent cartilage.

series.label: RT COR FSE T2 Fat Sat

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Anterior labral tear (arrow), with intact periosteum (arrow with annotation). Importantly, the labrum is not displaced. There is extension of the tear to the superior labrum in this case as well.
  • Hill Sachs lesion (small wedge shaped defect at / above coracoid - recall the humeral head should be round on the top few slices on axial)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Perthes lesion is a variant of an anteroinferior labral injury that occurs after anterior shoulder instability. In this lesion, the anteroinferior labrum is torn, but periosteum remains attached (unlike a classic Bankart). The labrum also remains in a near-normal anatomic position (unlike an ALPSA lesion). Because there is little or no displacement, Perthes lesions can be easily missed on routine MRI.

MRI Appearance:

A Perthes lesion appears as detachment of the anteroinferior labrum with preserved labral position at the glenoid rim. The normal triangular labral morphology may be maintained, and there is often minimal or no interposed fluid, which is why these lesions are frequently undercalled. The intact periosteum tethers the labrum to the rim, preventing medial displacement.

Thus, MR arthrography is particularly valuable for detecting Perthes lesions. Intra-articular contrast can subtly outline the labral detachment, showing contrast tracking between the labrum and glenoid even when the labrum looks anatomically positioned. This increased sensitivity is why Perthes lesions are more confidently diagnosed on MRA than on standard MRI.

  • ALPSA (Anterior Labroligamentous Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) - detachment of anteroinferior labrum, but labrum remains attached to periosteum. The detached labrum rotates medially to the anterior scapular neck.
  • **Perthes Lesion - less common - labrum detaches from glenoid but remains attached to periosteum, AND is not displaced (only minimal stripping). These may be occult on MR, and can spontaneously heal. Think of them as ALPSAs but without medial displacement.

cases.clinicalHistory: Recurrent shoulder subluxations/dislocations

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Anteroinferior labral tear, with an intact periosteum. The labrum is separate from the anterior glenoid rim, with contrast noted between. The labral tear itself with the intact periosteum can be labelled a Perthe’s lesion.
  • Cartilage delamination adjacent to the labral tear. Cartilage is undergoing delamination, ie; a cartilage injury has lifted the cartilage from the underlying bone and contrast is able to get between the cartilage and the bone.
  • Subchondral marrow changes underneath the cartilage injury.

Other Findings:

  • Small Hill-Sachs
  • Other labral findings include a sublabral recess, a tiny tear at 6 to 7 o'clock

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
GLAD lesion (GlenoLabral Articular Disruption) is an anteroinferior labral tear (classically non-displaced) associated with adjacent glenoid articular cartilage damage. Intra-articular bodies are common as a result.

MR Findings:

  • GLAD lesion appears as a tear of the anteroinferior labrum (classic = relatively non-displaced / superficial) with associated adjacent focal abnormality (thinning, irregularity, or defect) of the anteroinferior glenoid articular cartilage, sometimes with underlying subchondral marrow edema.
  • MR arthrography can improve detection by outlining subtle labral surface tears and better defining the extent of the cartilage defect.

You should always actively evaluate the glenoid cartilage whenever you see an anteroinferior labral abnormality.

The classic clinical history is pain-dominant -- and traditionally with NO instability. However, modern data indicate GLAD can coexist with or follow instability / dislocation. Still, think of GLAD as a "pain focused chondrolabral injury".

Note that a classic/true "GLAD" is pretty rare (i.e. our case is more a Perthes lesion with adjacent cartilage abnormality "GLAD-like"). With more severe labral abnormalities like a Bankart with adjacent cartilage injury -- don't call it a GLAD lesion. Consider this a Bankart + adjacent cartilage injury.
