Cor T1 FS post contrast DR
Cor T2 FS DR post contrast
Ax Loc Lg FOV post contrast RIGHT

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Superior labral tear extending from superior labrum, and posteriorly to the posterior labrum. You can see that the contrast cleft goes behind the biceps anchor, which is abnormal (not seen in sublabral recess). There is also increased signal in the superior labrum with irregularity, and a wider cleft than expected for a simple recess.

This is consistent with a SLAP Tear. We discuss classification in the learning material - but for now, don’t get caught in the different types of SLAP tears. Just describe the tear, parts of labrum involved, and where it extends into.

  • Anteroinferior labrum appears thickened, rounded. This likely represents a remote, healed, scarred tear. You can the labrum has lost its normal triangular, low signal morphology.
  • Supraspinatus tear. There is contrast entering the articular side fibres, which confirms an articular sided tear. This tear also involves the anterior fibres of infraspinatus tendon.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A SLAP tear refers to a superior labral tear that extends anterior to posterior relative to the biceps anchor. SLAP tears involve the superior labrum and often at long head of the biceps tendon origin. SLAP tears are an important cause of shoulder pain, especially in overhead athletes.

Calling a superior labral tear a "SLAP tear" should be restricted to young patients, ideally below 40 years of age. Degenerative superior labral changes are common, particularly in older patients, and should not be overcalled as clinically significant SLAP tears.

MR Appearance and Planes:

  • Abnormal high signal cleft within the superior labrum/biceps anchor that reaches the articular surface - best seen on coronal images
  • Often see irregular labral margins or labral detachment at the biceps anchor
  • Tear may tracking anterior and/or posterior from the anchor (axial images help confirm this)

MR arthrography improves detection of SLAP tears, particularly subtle or nondisplaced lesions.

  • Intra-articular contrast can outline labral detachment and clearly show contrast extending beneath the superior labrum or into the biceps anchor.
  • Multiple studies have shown higher sensitivity and specificity for MR arthrography compared with conventional MRI in diagnosing SLAP lesions, especially in younger patients and athletes.

SLAP tears are frequently associated with biceps tendinosis or partial tearing, rotator cuff pathology, and paralabral cysts. In overhead athletes, they may coexist with internal impingement findings, including posterior superior labral changes and undersurface cuff fraying.

Pitfalls:

The most common pitfall is confusing normal anatomic variants with pathology. A sublabral recess or sublabral foramen can mimic a tear but typically has smooth margins, predictable location, thinner (<2-3 mm), and does not extend posterior to the biceps anchor.

The key finding to identify is its relationship to the biceps anchor. Identify the biceps anchor on both the axial and coronal images, and correlate with the sagittal images. If the tear cleft goes posterior to the biceps anchor, it is not a normal variant.

teachingSections.name: Classifying SLAP Tears
teachingSections.html:
Many classifications exist, with many (10) types of SLAP lesions described. However, the most commonly used system has four major types. More important than memorizing is understanding the principle: All SLAP lesions involve superior labrum at LHBT origin, but extend into different structures from there. Most reports just describe which structures are involved rather than actually 'typing' them.

  • Type 1: Fraying only (NO real tear, labrum remains attached but with irregular margin and mild signal increase) - degenerative / overhead microtrauma.
  • Type 2: Stripping of frayed labrum from glenoid (increased signal/tear btw labrum and glenoid) - this is most common. There are subtypes based on what parts of labrum are involved (anterior, posterior, both)
  • Type 3: Bucket-handle tear - tear extends into superior labrum with the central fragment displaced towards the joint. See image example with arrowhead the central fragment. Tear does NOT involve LHBT.
  • Type 4: Bucket-handle tear PLUS extension into LHBT**

There are other types but beyond the scope here. It's useful to be aware that a system exists to know where to look. BUT, for real reporting, just describe the parts of labrum involved, the orientation, and other structures involved (especially LHBT if so).

cases.clinicalHistory: Posterior shoulder pain and feeling of instability

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Posteroinferior labral tear (6 to 9 o'clock). Increased signal cleft between the posterior labrum and the underlying glenoid. You shouldn’t see any increased signal cleft there as the labrum should normally be firmly against the underlying glenoid.
  • In contrast to the above tear, compare to the normal posterosuperior glenoid labrum.
  • The tear is isolated to the labrum, without any associated periosteal or capsular tear.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Posteroinferior labral tears are seen in patients with posterior instability, much less common than anteroinferior tears (as anterior instability is more common). We introduce the spectrum of posterior labral tears in the next section below.

Posterior tears are associated with posterior shoulder instability, trauma such as seizures or electric shock, or repetitive posterior loading seen in athletes like weightlifters and football linemen.

Imaging Planes:

  • Axial images are the most important plane for evaluation and should be carefully scrolled from superior to inferior along the posterior glenoid.
  • Even if you don’t see a discrete labral tear cleft, especially on non-arthrographic MRI, presence of paralabral cysts along the posterior glenoid are a helpful secondary sign and strongly suggest an underlying tear.
  • MR arthrography improves detection of posterior labral tears, particularly subtle or nondisplaced lesions. Intra-articular contrast can clearly outline labral detachment and is especially useful when conventional MRI findings are equivocal.

teachingSections.name: Intro: Posterior Labral Lesions
teachingSections.html:
We covered anteroinferior labral tears (Bankart and related lesions), and superior labral abnormalities (SLAP tears) in previous cases. Here we introduce Posteroinferior Labral Abnormalities. Anterior instability is way more common than posterior, so posteroinferior lesions are rare / much less common. Glenoid dysplasia predisposes patients to posterior instability (see below). Named labroligamentous lesions are analogous to their anterior counterparts.

To introduce named lesions:

  • Simple Posterior Labral Tear - isolated tear of posterior labrum without periosteal or capsular tear. [LIKE OUR CASE]
  • Reverse Bankart - similar to Bankart but posterior (6 to 10 o'clock), with detachment of the labrum (and periosteum no longer attached). Here's another example. Reverse osseous bankart involves fracture of posterior glenoid (avulsion or larger fracture).
  • POLPSA (Posterior Labrocapsular Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) - a reverse version of ALPSA, with posterior labrum and periosteum stripped and displaced, but the periosteum is still intact - resulting in a posterior recess that communicates with joint. Here's another example that better shows the posterior recess.
  • Posterior GLAD - same as anterior version with subtle tear + cartilage defect (but 7 to 9 o'clock)

Other Posterior Lesions:

  • Kim's Lesion - deep but incomplete tear of posteroinferior labrum between labrum and cartilage -- that looks subtle / 'concealed' ('marginal crack') superficially, but the deep labrum is clearly detached from glenoid rim. See this image example and read my description carefully - it sounds complex but its not.
  • Bennett Lesion - extra-articular curvilinear calcification/ ossificiation along posterior glenoid, due to traction injury of posterior capsule (IGHL / inferior glenohumeral ligament). It is associated with posterior labral tears and prior subluxation.

Overall, it's worth being aware of these and understanding what differentiates them. But, in real life, you will characterize the labral abnormality and revisit this material if needed.

Glenoid Dysplasia (Posterior glenoid rim deficiency) is an anatomic variant that predisposes patients to posterior instability. This appears as rounding of the posterior glenoid bone, with thickened posterior labral tissue to help compensate. This image shows examples varying in severity.

cases.clinicalHistory: Recurrent anterior shoulder dislocations. Humeral abnormality?
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 18 - Hill Sachs lesion after dislocation

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large Hill-Sachs lesion. (Also on sagittal) - wedge shaped defect at upper slices of humeral head (at or above the coracoid)
  • Mildly displaced anteroinferior glenoid labral tear. You can see the labrum has lost its normal triangular morphology and is mildly separated off the glenoid rim. This labral tear extends into superior labrum back to about 10 o'clock position. Biceps anchor is intact.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
While we have shown cases of anterior instability and associated labral findings in prior cases, we use this case for a dedicated discussion of Hill-Sachs lesions.

A Hill-Sachs lesion is an impaction fracture of the posterolateral humeral head that occurs during anterior shoulder dislocation, when the humeral head impacts the anteroinferior glenoid rim.

Imaging Planes:

  • Axial images are the most useful plane for identifying and characterizing a Hill-Sachs lesion
  • Hill-Sachs lesion is located in the upper 4-5 mm of humerus (normally round), at or above the coracoid in the axial plane.
  • If you are below this point / the humeral head equator, you run the risk of falsely diagnosing a Hill-Sachs lesion due to the natural concavity of the posterior cortex of the proximal humerus at this location.
  • Sagittal images can provide an overview of lesion depth and relationship to the articular surface.

On MRI, a Hill-Sachs lesion appears as a cortical depression or defect along the posterolateral humeral head:

  • Acute lesions show surrounding marrow edema
  • Chronic lesions appear well defined, sclerotic, and lack edema.
  • Report size (width, length, depth) - use axial slice where widest for width / depth, and sag for height.

Hill-Sachs lesions are frequently associated with anterior labral injuries such as Bankart, ALPSA, or Perthes lesions, as well as capsular injury and joint effusion. These were discussed in detail in previous cases.

They tend to grow with recurrent dislocations.

cases.clinicalHistory: Baseball player with chronic shoulder pain

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bennett lesion of the shoulder. Adjacent to the posterior labrum, you see foci of very low signal intensity which suggests mineralization. This is very closely related to the joint capsule.

Don’t worry about these streaks of fluid signal intensity within the muscle anteriorly (on T2W images). That is the from the local anesthetic injected while performing an MRI arthrogram. Note that you can only see them on T2 weighted images.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Bennett lesion refers to a ossification or calcification along the posterior glenoid rim, most commonly seen in overhead throwing athletes. It represents a traction-related injury at the posterior band of the inferior glenohumeral ligament and the posterior joint capsule, and is associated with repetitive posterior capsular stress rather than acute trauma. It is associated with labral tears / subluxation.

Imaging Plane:

Axial images are the most useful for identifying a Bennett lesion, particularly along the posteroinferior glenoid rim. This is how it is classically shown on exams.

Imaging Findings:

  • On MRI, a Bennett lesion appears as a low-signal intensity ossific or calcific focus along the posteroinferior glenoid margin, often contiguous with the posterior capsule or inferior glenohumeral ligament attachment.
  • There may be adjacent capsular thickening, posterior labral fraying, or reactive marrow changes.
  • CT is more sensitive for defining the extent and morphology of ossification (see this example), but MRI is valuable for assessing associated soft tissue / labral injury.
  • Plain radiographs in axial view may also show the mineralization along the posterior glenoid rim.

Bennett lesions are commonly associated with posterior labral tears, posterior capsular thickening, internal impingement, and undersurface rotator cuff fraying, particularly of the infraspinatus. In throwers, they may coexist with SLAP tears and other overuse-related findings.

A Bennett lesion in a thrower should prompt a careful search for posterior labral pathology and internal impingement findings. While the lesion itself may not always require treatment, its presence provides important context for the overall instability and pain pattern.

teachingSections.name: Review: Posterior Labral Lesions
teachingSections.html:
Here we review Posteroinferior Labral Abnormalities. Anterior instability is way more common than posterior, so posteroinferior lesions are rare / much less common. Glenoid dysplasia predisposes patients to posterior instability. Named labroligamentous lesions are analogous to their anterior counterparts.

To introduce named lesions:

  • Simple Posterior Labral Tear - isolated tear of posterior labrum without periosteal or capsular tear.
  • Reverse Bankart - similar to Bankart but posterior (6 to 10 o'clock), with detachment of the labrum (and periosteum no longer attached). Here's another example. Reverse osseous bankart involves fracture of posterior glenoid (avulsion or larger fracture).
  • POLPSA (Posterior Labrocapsular Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) - a reverse version of ALPSA, with posterior labrum and periosteum stripped and displaced, but the periosteum is still intact - resulting in a posterior recess that communicates with joint. Here's another example that better shows the posterior recess.
  • Posterior GLAD - same as anterior version with subtle tear + cartilage defect (but 7 to 9 o'clock)

Other Posterior Lesions:

  • Kim's Lesion - deep but incomplete tear of posteroinferior labrum between labrum and cartilage -- that looks subtle / 'concealed' ('marginal crack') superficially, but the deep labrum is clearly detached from glenoid rim. See this image example and read my description carefully - it sounds complex but its not.
  • ***Bennett Lesion - extra-articular curvilinear calcification/ ossificiation along posterior glenoid, due to traction injury of posterior capsule (IGHL / inferior glenohumeral ligament). It is associated with posterior labral tears and prior subluxation.

Overall, it's worth being aware of these and understanding what differentiates them. But, in real life, you will characterize the labral abnormality and revisit this material if needed.

Glenoid Dysplasia (Posterior glenoid rim deficiency) is an anatomic variant that predisposes patients to posterior instability. This appears as rounding of the posterior glenoid bone, with thickened posterior labral tissue to help compensate. This image shows examples varying in severity.

cases.clinicalHistory: History of shoulder instability and dislocation
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 20 - Reverse Hill Sachs and Posterior Labral Abnormalities

data.text: Periosteum stripped off the glenoid margin

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Labral tear involving the posteroinferior labrum
  • Reverse Hill Sachs lesion - there is flattening and indentation of anterior aspect of humeral head (reverse hill-sachs, due to prior posterior dislocation).
  • Supraspinatus tendinosis. Increased signal of the anterior fibres of supraspinatus tendon, but not fluid signal, so not a tear. Subscapularis tendinosis also present.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Posterior shoulder dislocation is an uncommon but frequently missed injury, particularly on plain radiography. It occurs in specific clinical scenario which includes seizures, electrical injury, or high-energy trauma. The classic injury pattern includes a reverse Hill-Sachs lesion of the humeral head and a reverse Bankart lesion involving the posterior labrum and glenoid.

In contrast to an anteroinferior glenohumeral joint dislocation, the humeral head dislocates straight posterior in a posterior glenohumeral joint dislocation. The location of the corresponding injuries reflect this direction.

A reverse Hill-Sachs lesion appears as an impaction fracture of the anteromedial humeral head, caused by engagement with the posterior glenoid rim. Acute lesions show marrow edema, whereas chronic lesions are well defined and sclerotic.

A reverse Bankart lesion involves detachment of the posterior labrum from the glenoid rim, sometimes with an associated posterior glenoid rim fracture. Fluid or contrast may be seen interposed between the labrum and glenoid.

cases.clinicalHistory: History of fall with shoulder injury

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• At site of inferior glenohumeral ligament, there is redundant ligament with surrounding increased signal. The ligament is torn, and does not attach to the medial neck of humerus. This is consistent with Humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament (HAGL)

Other Findings:

  • Fracture of the greater tuberosity.
  • Complete traumatic tear of supraspinatus tendon with retraction.
  • Complete tear of infraspinatus tendon with retraction.
  • High grade near complete tearing of teres minor
  • Tendinopathy with partial tearing of subscapularis, but grossly intact
  • Long head of biceps tendon tear. Empty bicipital groove.
  • Extensive edema and hemorrhage seen of the joint capsule and throughout the subacromial subdeltoid bursa. Moderate sized glenohumeral joint effusion.
  • No discrete labral tear. No bankart.

Findings relate to an acute fracture-dislocation at glenohumeral joint. This includes complete rotator cuff tears, greater tuberosity fracture, LHBT tear, HAGL lesion d/t anterior dislocation.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
HAGL is an underappreciated instability lesion. Recall, the Inferior Glenohumeral Ligament (IGHL) is an important stabilizer preventing anterior dislocation. Normally, this attaches from inferior labrum to neck of humerus (is sling-like; anterior band attaches to anteroinferior labrum, posterior to posteroinferior labrum, and an axillary recess between).

With anterior instability:

  • IGHL failure at the labral attachment produces anteroinferior labral abnormalities we discussed (i.e. Bankart and related lesions) = common.
  • Less common, failure/tear at the humeral attachment can occur and produces a HAGL (this case) = Humeral Avulsion of the inferior Glenohumeral Ligament.

HAGL lesions matter because they are a known cause of failed instability surgery if unrecognized. If only the labrum is repaired and the humeral-sided ligament avulsion is missed, instability can persist.

Imaging Planes:

  • Coronal oblique images are the most helpful plane for detecting HAGL lesions and should be carefully reviewed at the level of the inferior glenohumeral ligament. Axial images provide confirmation and help assess the anterior or posterior extent of the injury.

MRI Appearance:

  • A HAGL lesion appears as discontinuity or nonvisualization of the inferior glenohumeral ligament at its humeral attachment.
  • One classic imaging feature is the J-shaped axillary recess, where the normally U-shaped inferior capsule becomes elongated and tapers toward the humerus.
  • You may see fluid extravasation along the humeral neck, and in some cases, a small osseous avulsion fragment at the ligament attachment.

MR arthrography improves detection of HAGL lesions by distending the joint and outlining capsular tears. Contrast extravasation can be seen along the humeral neck or outside the joint capsule, making the ligament avulsion more conspicuous.

HAGL lesions often coexist with other instability findings, including Bankart or ALPSA lesions, Hill-Sachs lesions, and capsular laxity.

cases.clinicalHistory: History of shoulder subluxations

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Circumferential glenoid labral tear. You can see that the glenoid labrum is not closely applied to the underlying glenoid. There is separation between the labrum and the glenoid. However, there is no contrast undercutting the separated labrum. That is due to the chronicity of the tear and scar/granulation tissue between the tear and the glenoid.
  • Also see tears affecting the superior and inferior labrum. Make sure you scroll through all the images to follow the labrum.

Other Subtle/more equivocal findings (not reason for showing this case):

  • Subtle flattening of posterosuperior humeral head, a subtle hill-sachs
  • Notice anteroinferior focal full thickness cartilage defect. There is also subtle bony flattening / impaction injury at anteroinferior glenoid rim.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A circumferential labral tear typically extending through the anterior, inferior, posterior, and often superior labrum. These injuries are most often seen in patients with recurrent instability (note evidence of instability in our case), high-energy trauma, or failed prior stabilization surgery.

On MRI, a circumferential labral tear appears as labral detachment or irregularity involving multiple regions. You may see fluid or contrast tracking along the labral-glenoid interface across anterior, posterior, inferior, and sometimes superior segments.

MR arthrography is particularly helpful, as intra-articular contrast can outline the full extent of labral detachment and confirm that abnormalities seen in different regions are connected rather than isolated findings.

Circumferential labral tears are commonly associated with capsular laxity, Hill-Sachs lesions, glenoid bone loss, and signs of chronic instability.

Remember the “satisfaction of search” principle. If you identify a labral tear, make sure the entirety of the labrum is evaluated carefully to make sure you are not missing labral tears at other locations.

cases.clinicalHistory: Chronic left shoulder injury. Limited painful range of motion.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Thickening and edema of the joint capsule at the axillary recess. Note the thickening and increased signal of the capsule and IGHL. The capsule should be no more than a pencil line thickness.
  • Increased signal within the rotator interval. Normal rotator interval should not have any increased signal on T2 weighted fat saturated images, as all the fat at the location should be saturated (dark),
  • Pericapsular edema, which can be best seen in sagittal images.
  • Evidence of remote labral tear anteroinferiorly. The anteroinferior labrum has lost its normal morphology, and is mildly displaced off the anterior glenoid.

Findings are typical of adhesive capsulitis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Adhesive capsulitis, commonly referred to as frozen shoulder, is a clinical diagnosis characterized by progressive pain and global restriction of both active and passive shoulder motion. The diagnosis is usually clinical, but, in equivocal clinical findings, MRI can be useful to help make the diagnosis.

MRI Findings:

Focus on the joint capsule and rotator interval:

  • Thickening of the joint capsule/synovium, particularly at the axillary recess (measuring > 4 mm is quite specific). This is combined capsule-synovial thickening acutely, and fibrotic thickening chronically.
  • Associated edema of IGHL.
  • Commonly, there is obliteration or loss of the normal axillary recess volume.
  • Thickening and increased signal within the rotator interval structures, including the coracohumeral ligament. Loss of fat in the rotator interval
  • Enhancement of the capsule and synovium may be seen on post-contrast imaging (though not often performed)

Associated findings may include mild joint effusion, synovitis, and edema around the rotator interval. The rotator cuff itself is typically intact, and the absence of a significant cuff tear supports the diagnosis when imaging findings are subtle.


Joint Capacity is Reduced (often < 7 cc) -- so during arthrogram it feels hard to inject fluid. Hydrodilation (forceful intra-articular injection of fluid) can be used to distend joint with a goal of improving pain / range of motion, but is not first line treatment (start with conservative, intra-articular steroid, PT, etc which helps most people).

cases.clinicalHistory: Tennis player with superior shoulder pain

series.label: Ax T1 Space FS post contrast
COR T2 FS post contrast rpt

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Articular sided partial tear (low grade) of the infraspinatus tendon. Also see on sagittal.
  • Posterosuperior glenoid labral tear. You can see an incomplete fluid cleft within the labrum. Some mild increased T2 signal in biceps tendon at base near origin.
  • Note the anatomic variant anterosuperior labrum (not a tear).
  • Associated bony changes. Subcortical cysts.

Constellation of findings raise suspicion for internal (posterosuperior) impingement.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Posterosuperior glenoid impingement, the most common type of internal impingement, occurs when the posterosuperior rotator cuff and labrum are compressed between the greater tuberosity of humerus and the posterosuperior glenoid when in ABER position (abduction and external rotation = throwing). This mechanism is classically seen in overhead throwing athletes and is a common cause of posterior shoulder pain in this population.

MRI Findings:

Posterosuperior glenoid impingement is characterized by:

  • Articular-sided fraying or partial-thickness tearing of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus (often the junctional fibres between the posterior supraspinatus and anterior infraspinatus)
  • Posterosuperior labral tearing or fraying, like in our case (SLAP-like tears, often posterior to biceps anchor)
  • Posterolateral bone changes, with subchondral edema or cystic changes of the adjacent humeral head

Presence of these three features (classic triad) should make posterosuperior glenoid impingement top of mind. ABER positioning at MRI can improv edetection.

Internal impingement often coexists with microinstability and capsular laxity -- best evaluated clinically. But, you may see signs of capsular redundancy (increased volume, patulous capsule -- ie the opposite of adhesive capsulitis). In our case we have a mildly capacious/enlarged anterior recess (capsular laxity). This is supportive but less important than the three findings listed above.

Knowing the patient’s clinical history, including any overhead activities including sports, can help with diagnosis. Always make it a habit to obtain more clinical history from the patient while you are injecting the patient for an MRI arthrogram.

cases.clinicalHistory: Persistent pain and decreased function, assess muscle
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 25 - Fatty Atrophy of rotator cuff muscles, secondary to massive rotator cuff tear

data.text: medially dislocated long head of biceps tendon

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate to severe fatty infiltration and volume loss of supraspinatus muscle. On the Y view, the supraspinatus muscle bulk falls below the tangent line drawn between the superior margin of coracoid and acromion. There is also fatty replacement within the muscle. T1 images are useful to assess muscle bulk and fatty replacement -- which can be easily missed on fat suppressed images.
  • Volume loss and fatty infiltration of the infraspinatus muscle, and subscapularis muscle (you can see the feathery appearance of the subscapularis muscle best on the axial images).

Massive Rotator Cuff Tear:

  • Complete tear of supraspinatus tendon with retraction. The arrow points to the torn tendon end.
  • Complete tear of the infraspinatus tendon with retraction.
  • Similar to another example shown previously, this case is another example of chronic complete tears of supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons -- scarring with each other to form a tendon loop.
  • Full thickness tear of subscapularis. Here is the bare footprint of the subscapularis tendon.
  • Medial dislocation of the long head of biceps tendon (remember, if the biceps tendon is not in the bcicpital groove, there should be a subscapularis tendon tear, until proven otherwise)
  • High riding humeral head remodeling the undersurface of the acromion (long arrows). Normally, the medal aspect of the humeral head and the inferior margin of the glenoid (short arrows) should be aligned against each other at the same level (here the humeral head is high riding).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We previously introduced muscle atrophy -- including assessment of muscle bulk and fatty infiltration (reviewed below).

Fatty infiltration of the rotator cuff muscles represents chronic muscle degeneration that occurs after long-standing abnormalities, most commonly in the setting of massive rotator cuff tears. It reflects irreversible muscle changes related to chronic denervation, disuse, and tendon retraction.

This finding is critical because muscle quality, not just tendon tear size, is one of the strongest predictors of surgical reparability and functional outcome.

Sagittal oblique T1-weighted images are the most important sequence for assessing muscle quality and should always be reviewed when evaluating a rotator cuff tear. This plane allows direct visualization of all four rotator cuff muscles within their respective fossae. The supraspinatus Y-view is a key landmark. Normally, the supraspinatus muscle should fill the supraspinatus fossa. Loss of bulk or increased fat signal relative to adjacent muscles should be reported.

teachingSections.name: Review: Fatty Infiltration and Atrophy
teachingSections.html:
Fatty Infiltration and Bulk are related but different. Both should be assessed.

Fatty infiltration is assessed using the Goutallier classification (not 'bulk'):

  • Grade 0 represents normal muscle with no fat
  • Grade 1 shows a few fatty streaks
  • Grade 2 has more muscle than fat
  • Grade 3 shows roughly equal fat and muscle
  • Grade 4 indicates more fat than muscle

In practical terms, people often consider grade 0-1 "normal", 2 moderate, and 3-4 advanced fatty infiltration.

As the Goutallier grade increases, it reflects more chronic disease and is associated with poorer surgical outcomes including higher retear rates. Fatty infiltration is often irreversible, even after successful tendon repair.

A k﻿ey landmark for supraspinatus muscle assessment is the oblique sagittal Y-view - for both fatty infiltration and bulk.

Muscle Bulk: Normally, the supraspinatus muscle bulk should fill the supraspinatus fossa. To be more precise, if you draw a line from upper scapular spine to upper coracoid, normally there should be muscle belly above the line. If not ('positive tangent sign'), consider it reduced bulk / atrophic (like our case).

Always compare with adjacent muscles and, when available, the contralateral side.

The trapezius muscle is often a good baseline to use as the patient's background muscle quality. Deltoid muscle can also be used, but can show atrophy or edema with chronic rotator cuff pathology.

cases.clinicalHistory: Acute shoulder weakness
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 26 - Suprascapular Nerve Entrapment (Spinoglenoid Notch Cyst)

series.label: COR OBL T2 FSE FATSAT LT
SAG OBL T2 FSE FATSAT LT

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• SLAP Tear: Superior glenoid labral tear extending both anteriorly and posteriorly. As an aside, we call the anterosuperior labral portion of this a tear (rather than a normal variant) because it is both contiguous with the rest of the SLAP tear, and the morphology (extending into labrum substance rather than along glenoid).
  • Paralabral cyst adjacent to the posterosuperior labral tear. You can see the cyst closely related to the posterosuperior labrum, suggesting its origin. The cyst extends to the spinoglenoid notch, adjacent to the suprascapular neurovascular bundle. Also see on sagittal, with the cyst in the spinoglenoid notch and the neurovacqlar bundle deep to it.
  • Intramuscular edema and volume loss of infraspinatus muscle. The rest of the cuff muscles maintain normal volume and signal. In particular, notice that the supraspinatus muscle is normal, which is also supplied by suprascapular nerve. We discuss why in the learning material.
  • Mild supraspinatus tendinosis
  • Unfused os acromiale. This can sometimes be cause of symptoms from micro motion between the fragments.

Findings are in keeping with suprascapular nerve entrapment at the spinoglenoid notch, due to a paralabral cyst. Typical denervation pattern.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Suprascapular nerve entrapment (suprascaplar neuropathy) is critical to recognize, and a very classic exam question. Take the time to understand the anatomy -- which makes things easy to understand.

Key Anatomy:

Recall that the suprascapular nerve arises from brachial plexus and travels over the scapula from anterior to posterior through two important named spaces: suprascapular notch first (proximal) --> innervates supraspinatus --> spinoglenoid notch (distal) --> innervates infraspinatus. Thus:

  • Compression at suprascapular notch (proximal/anterior) = denervation of BOTH supraspinatus AND infraspinatus
  • Compression at spinoglenoid notch (our case, posterior) = denervation of infraspinatus ONLY.

The classic exam question is to show this image only (edema +/- fatty infiltration if chronic). Nerve compression is often caused by a space-occupying lesion.


Spinoglenoid notch entrapment is most commonly caused by a paralabral cyts resulting from a SLAP tear (like our case exactly). The tear acts as a one-way valve, allowing joint fluid to track out and form a cyst.

The nerve compression can lead to pain and, more importantly, selective muscle denervation. If unrecognized, patients may develop progressive weakness and irreversible muscle atrophy, even if the labral tear itself appears relatively small.


MRI Findings:

  • A paralabral cyst appears as a well-defined fluid-signal collection adjacent to the glenoid, most commonly posterior or posterosuperior
  • The cyst may extend into the spinoglenoid notch and cause mass effect on the suprascapular nerve, like in our case.
  • Associated posterior or posterosuperior labral tear should be actively looked for, as treating the labral tear is key to preventing cyst recurrence.

Denervation changes (in the infraspinatus muscle in this case) are a critical secondary finding:

  • Early denervation manifests as increased signal on fluid-sensitive sequences due to edema.
  • Chronic compression leads to fatty infiltration / atrophy of the muscle on T1-weighted images.
  • The distribution helps localize the level of nerve compression, as described above.

Pearl: Always evaluate "special spaces" on shoulder MRI as a routine part of your approach, including along the path of the suprascapular nerve (suprascapular and spinoglenoid notch).

cases.clinicalHistory: Progressive weakness of shoulder

series.label: COR OBL T2 FSE FATSAT LT
SAG OBL T2 FSE FATSAT LT

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Generalized edema (increased signal on T2 weighted images) of supraspinatus, infraspinatus and teres minor muscles. Compare the signal to the rest of the muscles. Also on coronal sequence (supraspinatus, infraspinatus and teres minor). You can see the teees minor is less well affected compared to the other two muscles.
  • Involvement of two different peripheral nerve distributions: the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles point to suprascaular nerve involvement, proximal to the supraspinatus innervation (ie suprascapular notch or proximal to it), and involvement of teres minor should point towards an axillary nerve involvement (usually at the quadrilateral space). No compressive lesions at either site to explain this.
  • Supraspinatus tendinosis with intrasubstance tear (you can see the tear does not extend to the bursar or articular surface)
  • Infraspinatus tendinosis. Thickening and heterogeneity of the tendon.

IMPRESSION:

Marked edema of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, and to a lesser extent teres minor. No visible compressive cause (the quadrilateral space, spinoglenoid notch, and suprascapular notch are unremarkable). This pattern suggests denervation edema in two nerve distributions, suggestive of a noncompressive brachial plexopathy (e.g. Parsonage-Turner syndrome).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Parsonage-Turner syndrome, also known as idiopathic brachial neuritis, is an acute inflammatory (immune-mediated) neuropathy that affects the brachial plexus or branches. Think of this when the muscles show denervation changes in 2+ nerve distributions (i.e. suprascapular and axillary) -- the classic way it is shown on exams. Most frequently involved muscles include: supra/infraspinatus (suprascapular nerve), teres minor/deltoid (axillary N), serratus anterior (long thoracic N).

It typically presents with sudden, severe shoulder or upper arm pain followed days to weeks later by weakness and muscle wasting.

MRI Findings:

Can mimic muscle changes seen in nerve entrapment syndromes and rotator cuff tears (misdiagnosis can lead to unnecessary surgery). Recognizing the characteristic muscle denervation pattern allows correct diagnosis and appropriate conservative management:

  • Acute: diffuse increased signal within affected muscles on fluid-sensitive sequences, reflecting denervation edema.
  • In subacute or chronic stages, the denervation edema may resolve and be replaced by muscle atrophy and fatty infiltration on T1-weighted images.
  • Often in 2+ peripheral nerve distributions (i.e. in this case, supra/infraspinatus and teres minor involved = suprascapular and axillary nerve territories both involved)
  • Excludes mimics: severe rotator cuff tears, nerve entrapment/compression, etc.

The absence of a mass or structural cause of nerve compression helps distinguish Parsonage-Turner syndrome from entrapment neuropathies such as spinoglenoid notch impingement. You don't usually identify a cause to explain the MRI findings.

Pitfalls:

A common pitfall is misinterpreting denervation edema as muscle strain, myositis, or postoperative change. Always correlate with clinical history of acute onset pain followed by weakness. When this pattern is seen, the report should clearly state that findings are most consistent with denervation and suggest Parsonage-Turner syndrome rather than primary shoulder pathology.

cases.clinicalHistory: Pain and hypermobility of joint

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Extensive glenoid labral tear, involving the inferior, posterior, and anteroinferior labrum.
  • Associated paralabral cysts
  • P﻿aralabral cyst extending to the quadrilateral space. Also see on non fat saturated image to better see the adjacent anatomy.
  • Streaky, subtle edema in teres minor muscle (denervation edema). Compare to the other muscles which do not show the increased signal.

Other Findings:

  • Supraspinatus tendinosis, with low grade partial articular sided tear.
  • ACJ osteoarthritis

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Quadrilateral space syndrome is a neurovascular compression syndrome involving the axillary nerve, and occasionally the posterior circumflex humeral artery, as they pass through the quadrilateral space. It most commonly affects young, active patients and overhead athletes and typically presents with poorly localized posterior shoulder pain, paresthesias, and weakness.

Key Anatomy: Quadrilateral Space

Bounded by:

  • Teres Minor (Superiorly)
  • Teres Major (Inferiorly)
  • Long Head of Triceps Tendon (Medial)
  • Humeral neck (Lateral)

Contents: Axillary Nerve and Circumflex humeral vessels.

MRI Findings:

  • Hallmark finding is abnormal signal in the teres minor muscle.
  • Signal abnormality of denervation (acute/subacute = denervation edema; chronic = fatty infiltration / atrophy on T1)
  • Teres minor involvement may be isolated or accompanied by changes in the deltoid, depending on the severity and extent of axillary nerve involvement
  • The quadrilateral space itself may appear narrowed or distorted, sometimes due to fibrous bands, hypertrophied muscles, paralabral cysts, or post-traumatic scarring, although a discrete compressive mass is often not visible.

Pitfalls:

  • Seeing isolated teres minor atrophy and calling it chronic rotator cuff disease or age-related change
  • Overlooking the quadrilateral space entirely and focusing only on the rotator cuff and labrum. Routine approach to shoulder MR should include quadrilateral space as one of the reviewed special spaces.

Isolated teres minor denervation is highly suggestive of quadrilateral space syndrome. When this pattern is seen, actively inspect the quadrilateral space on axial images for subtle narrowing or mass effect and correlate with clinical symptoms.

cases.clinicalHistory: Fall from bike. Pain and unstable. History of gym injury years ago.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Osteoarthritis of the glenohumeral joint: characterized by cartilage loss, subchondral cystic changes, subchondral marrow edema (increased signal on T2 weighted images) and marginal osteophyte formation.
  • Posterior glenoid labral tear
  • Degenerative superior glenoid labrum (increased signal of labrum with loss of normal triangular morphology, but no fluid signal to call a tear)
  • Partial thickness articular sided supraspinatus tendon tear (also on coronal)
  • Intra articular long head of biceps tendinosis (the tendon has increased signal within, should be uniformly dark)
  • ACJ OA

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease affecting the articular cartilage of the humeral head and glenoid in the setting of an intact or relatively preserved rotator cuff. It is much less common than hip or knee osteoarthritis and often presents with deep shoulder pain, stiffness, and loss of range of motion.

This distinction matters because glenohumeral osteoarthritis has different imaging features, biomechanics, and management compared with rotator cuff arthropathy. Preserved rotator cuff integrity supports standard arthroplasty options, whereas cuff deficiency fundamentally alters surgical planning. Hence, MRI is essential not just to evaluate the OA, but also to assess the integrity of the cuff.

MRI Findings:

Glenohumeral osteoarthritis is characterized by:

  • Focal or diffuse cartilage thinning or full-thickness cartilage loss involving the humeral head and/or glenoid
  • Associated joint space narrowing
  • Marginal osteophyte formation
  • Subchondral marrow changes, including sclerosis and cystic change
  • Loose bodies may be present in more advanced disease

Rotator cuff tendons are typically intact or show only mild age-related tendinosis without full-thickness tearing. This is the main distinction between primary OA, and rotator cuff arthropathy (refer to case on rotator cuff arthropathy -- i.e. massive rotator cuff tears).

Associated findings commonly include joint effusion, synovial thickening, and degenerative labral tearing, particularly involving the superior and posterior labrum. Posterior glenoid wear and humeral head subluxation may be present in advanced disease.

MRI is particularly useful for early or moderate disease when radiographs may appear deceptively normal. A report on MRI of the shoulder should clearly make the distinction between primary glenohumeral OA and rotator cuff arthropathy.

cases.clinicalHistory: Shoulder pain
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 30 - Focal chondral defect with loose body

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Focal chondral defect posterior glenoid (follow the glenoid cartilage, by windowing it, and follow the normal concave shape of the labrum, to be familiar with it. The defect is along the posterior glenoid)
  • Loose chondral body in the joint space. Note how the body is in the dependent portion of the joint

Other Findings:

  • Superior labral tear with extension to anterior labrum and the posterior labrum. The posterior labrum has excess soft tissue suggestive of a healed tear.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A focal chondral defect occurs when there is loss of articular cartilage involving the humeral head or glenoid (due to focal cartilage injury), rather than diffuse cartilage degeneration as seen in osteoarthritis. When a fragment detaches, it can form a loose chondral body within the glenohumeral joint.

Focal cartilage injury can be a significant pain generator even when the rest of the joint appears relatively preserved. Loose chondral bodies may cause mechanical symptoms such as catching, locking, or episodic pain, and they can accelerate secondary degenerative change if not recognized.

MRI Findings:

  • Focal chondral defect appears as a well-defined area of cartilage thinning or full-thickness cartilage loss, often with exposed subchondral bone
  • Adjacent marrow edema may be present in acute or symptomatic lesions
  • A loose chondral body appears as a small intra-articular fragment that is isointense to cartilage, typically low signal on T1 and intermediate signal on fluid-sensitive sequences.
  • It does not demonstrate the low signal rim seen with ossified loose bodies.
  • An Acute loose body will have similar size to the defect (will fit well to the dimensions of the chondral defect), but over time, they may imbibe synovial fluid and become larger, or fragment and reduce in size.

Make it a habit in every case to recognize loose bodies in typical locations, especially the axillary recess and along the biceps tendon sheath. If a loose body is identified, always look for and describe the corresponding cartilage defect.

cases.clinicalHistory: Recurrent shoulder pain and effusion

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Multiple bodies with the glenohumeral joint, biceps interval and subcoracoid recess
  • Deformity of the humeral head, with osteophytes.
  • Deformed glenoid, and full thickness cartilage loss
  • Supraspinatus low grade bursal sided tear

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Synovial Osteochondromatosis (SOC) is a benign synovial disorder where joint lining undergoes cartilaginous metaplasia -- forming many nodules that detach as loose bodies. The bodies can be purely cartilaginous, calcified, or ossified. Knee most common site > Hip > Shoulder Elbow Wrist Ankle.

"Primary" SOC = idiopathic, with numerous similar sized nodules. Joint cartilage maintained early, but longstanding can lead to OA [OUR CASE].

"Secondary" SOC = another joint insult (OA, trauma) --> fewer, larger, variable bodies with background of degenerative change.

MRI Findings:

  • Multiple intra-articular nodules, which may be purely cartilaginous, partially calcified, or ossified depending on disease stage.
  • Cartilaginous nodules are similar in signal to cartilage
  • Ossified bodies demonstrate central fatty marrow signal (see FS) with a low-signal cortical rim.
  • Calcified nodules (chondroid calcifications) will show signal loss on all series.
  • Often associated joint effusion and synovial thickening.
  • Articular cartilage may be preserved early but can show secondary degenerative changes in longstanding disease.

Associated findings may include capsular distension, subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, and secondary osteoarthritic change. The loose bodies often collect in the axillary recess, subscapularis recess, and biceps tendon sheath.

Pitfalls:

A common pitfall is confusing synovial osteochondromatosis with degenerative loose bodies from osteoarthritis. The key distinction is the presence of numerous similarly sized loose bodies and prominent synovial proliferation rather than marginal osteophytes and focal cartilage loss. Non-calcified cartilaginous bodies will not be seen on radiographs, making MRI particularly valuable. Here's the classic appearance on Xray (a classic exam Q).

Reporting Tip:

Describe in detail the types of bodies and the distribution of loose bodies rather than just stating their presence. When multiple loose bodies are seen without advanced osteoarthritis, synovial osteochondromatosis should be strongly considered.

cases.clinicalHistory: Recurrent joint effusion and pain

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Erosive changes of the humeral head and posterior glenoid
  • Synovial thickening with focal modularity, within the subcoracoid recess and posterior aspect of glenohumeral joint. The synovial proliferation is low signal which is characteristic for PVNS.
  • Moderate degenerative changes of the glenohumeral joint
  • Supraspinatus tendinosis

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS) is a benign synovial proliferative disorder characterized by synovial hypertrophy -- often nodular and can be locally aggressive. The hallmark is hemosiderin deposition. We introduced PVNS in detail in the Knee MRI course.

PVNS can mimic inflammatory arthritis, infection, or nonspecific synovitis, yet management is different and often surgical.

MRI Findings:

  • Characterized by diffuse or nodular synovial thickening with low to intermediate signal on both T1- and T2-weighted images due to hemosiderin (the hallmark).
  • Blooming artifact on gradient-echo sequences is a key diagnostic feature (see Knee MR course, not performed in this case).
  • No calcifications within on Xray
  • Joint effusion is common, and pressure erosions or scalloping of adjacent bone may be seen in chronic cases ["extrinsic erosion of bone"]
  • Articular cartilage may be preserved early but can be secondarily damaged with longstanding disease. In advanced cases, there may be secondary OA due to chronic synovial inflammation.

A common pitfall is mistaking PVNS for inflammatory synovitis when low T2 signal and blooming are not actively sought. Another pitfall is overlooking subtle bone erosions, which support a chronic proliferative process rather than simple effusion.

cases.clinicalHistory: Increasing shoulder pain and swelling, and feeling unwell

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large joint effusion in glenohumeral joint, with internal debris
  • Humeral head marrow edema, with focal erosion. Edema in greater tuberosity
  • Diffuse edema of all the adjacent muscles, as well as subcutaneous edema

Other Findings:

  • Lipoma within long head of triceps muscle
  • Subscapularis tendon tear
  • Medial dislocation of long head of biceps tendon

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Septic arthritis of the shoulder is an orthopedic and radiologic emergency. It is a clinical diagnosis, but typical imaging findings should make you consider infection first given urgency.

Infection of the glenohumeral joint can rapidly destroy cartilage and spread to adjacent bone, resulting in osteomyelitis if not promptly treated. Missed or delayed diagnosis can lead to irreversible joint destruction, chronic pain, and functional loss.

MRI Appearance:

  • Septic arthritis typically presents as a large joint effusion with synovial thickening and intense synovial enhancement.
  • The synovium may appear irregular and hyperemic rather than smooth.
  • When infection extends into bone, osteomyelitis appears as marrow edema with low T1 and high T2 signal, often with enhancement and possible cortical erosion or subchondral bone destruction.
  • Adjacent articular cartilage may be thinned or disrupted in more advanced cases.

Important Associated findings may include **surrounding soft tissue edema**, subacromial–subdeltoid bursitis, abscess formation, and reactive lymphadenopathy. In postoperative or post-injection cases, infection may track along surgical planes or needle paths.

Pitfalls:

  • A major pitfall is not considering septic arthritis first in patients with this appearance (instead, favoring inflammatory arthritis or crystal disease when clinical information is limited).
  • Another pitfall is underestimating early osteomyelitis, which may present only as subtle marrow edema without overt cortical destruction. Marrow edema adjacent to an infected joint should always raise concern for osteomyelitis.
  • Importantly, diffuse synovial enhancement and aggressive soft tissue edema favor infection over degenerative or inflammatory causes.

When septic arthritis is suspected, the report should clearly state concern for infection and recommend urgent clinical correlation and aspiration (to make the diagnosis).

cases.clinicalHistory: Chronic monoarthritis. Progressive joint destruction on radiographs.

series.label: AX T1 LT POST GAD FS
Cor T1 POST GAD FS LT

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Shoulder joint effusion with synovial thickening (see the thickening and increased signal of the white line that is the synovium)
  • Enhancement of the thickened synovium (post contrast enhanced T1W FS image)
  • Collapse and destruction of the humeral head, with associated marrow edema. (note the humeral head has lost its normal spherical shape)
  • Erosive changes of the glenoid (see how the glenoid is markedly destroyed and eroded to its base, and also see destruction on this T1W image of the humeral head as well)

The findings indicate a destructive joint-centered process.

Other Findings:

  • Tendinosis of supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons. (It is very difficult to follow the tendons in this case, due to the deformed morphology of the glenohumeral joint)
  • Subscapularis tendinosis
  • Intramuscular edema involving the muscles (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, subscapularis). This is most likely secondary to disuse.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Hydroxyapatite Deposition Disease (HADD) commonly involves periarticular deposition of calcium hydroxyapatite -- most commonly calcific tendinitis (i.e. of supraspinatus).

Milwaukee shoulder, caused by intra-articular deposition of hydroxyapatite, is its most aggressive form -- involving destructive arthropathy. Imaging appearance alone is not reliably differentiable from infection, but clinical history, time-course, and some imaging clues can be seen. Joint aspiration should be performed.

It typically affects elderly patients and presents with chronic shoulder pain, swelling, and progressive loss of function. The condition is characterized by rapid joint destruction in the absence of inflammatory arthritis or infection.

Milwaukee shoulder can mimic septic arthritis or advanced osteoarthritis on imaging, yet management is different.

MRI Findings:

Milwaukee shoulder typically shows:

  • Large glenohumeral joint effusion with synovial thickening and debris
  • Marked destruction of the articular surfaces, often with flattening or collapse of the humeral head and glenoid
  • The rotator cuff is usually severely degenerated or torn, often with massive cuff deficiency, which contributes to abnormal joint mechanics. This can be a clue -- including high-riding humerus.
  • Intra-articular loose bodies and capsular distension may be present.
  • Associated findings often include subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, periarticular soft tissue calcifications, and advanced rotator cuff tearing.

IMPORTANTLY: Despite the dramatic imaging appearance mimicking infection, systemic signs of infection are typically absent, which helps distinguish this entity from septic arthritis clinically. Again, the imaging can look identical so infection should always be considered first.

Pitfall:

  • A common clinical pitfall is misdiagnosing Milwaukee shoulder as infection when a large effusion and synovitis are present. Still, we should consider infection first based on this imaging appearance.
  • Another pitfall is labeling the findings as end-stage osteoarthritis without acknowledging the crystal-induced destructive pattern.

Therefore, think of Milwaukee shoulder in an elderly patient with rapid, progressive joint destruction. Of course, septic arthritis should also be on your mind, and needs to be clinically correlated.

cases.clinicalHistory: Clinical history of fall off bike

series.label: COR OBL T2 FSE FATSAT RIGHT
SAG OBL T2 FSE FATSAT RIGHT

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Incongruity of the acromioclavicular joint with cranial displacement of the clavicle in relation to the acromion (following the undersurface of the acromion should normally be a straight line to the distal clavicle across the joint)
  • Associated edema of the clavicle and disruption of the joint capsule
  • Edema within the coracoclavicular ligaments

Other Findings:

  • L﻿ong head of biceps tendinosis
  • Superior glenoid labral tear
  • Partial thickness bursal sided tear of supraspinatus tendon (also see coronal)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Acromioclavicular joint dislocation results from disruption of the stabilizing ligaments of the AC joint, most commonly due to a direct blow to the shoulder or a fall onto the lateral aspect of the arm. The injury spectrum ranges from mild ligament sprain to complete disruption with marked clavicular displacement.

On MRI, key features include widening of the AC joint, elevation or displacement of the distal clavicle, disruption of the acromioclavicular ligaments, and injury to the coracoclavicular ligaments. Associated marrow edema, and surrounding soft tissue swelling may be present in acute injuries.


Rockwood classification of ACJ injury:

  • Rockwood type I represents a sprain of the acromioclavicular ligaments with normal joint alignment and intact coracoclavicular ligaments. Imaging may be normal or show mild capsular edema.
  • Rockwood type II involves complete disruption of the acromioclavicular ligaments with partial injury to the coracoclavicular ligaments. There is mild AC joint widening with minimal clavicular elevation.
  • Rockwood type III includes complete disruption of both the acromioclavicular and coracoclavicular ligaments. The distal clavicle is displaced superiorly, typically 25 to 100 percent increase in coracoclavicular (CC) distance.
  • Rockwood type IV is characterized by posterior displacement of the distal clavicle into or through the trapezius muscle, best appreciated on axial images.
  • Rockwood type V represents a more severe form of type III injury, with marked superior displacement of the distal clavicle, usually greater than 100 percent increase in CC distance, and extensive soft tissue disruption.
  • Rockwood type VI is rare and involves inferior displacement of the distal clavicle, often beneath the coracoid or acromion, and is usually associated with high-energy trauma.

A common pitfall is underestimating injury severity by relying only on coronal images and missing posterior displacement on axial views. Make sure the axial images are evaluated to ensure the clavicle is aligned against the acromion and not posteriorly displaced (in this case, the technologist has clipped the superior images of the axial plane, hence, the cranially displaced clavicle has not been completely imaged).

cases.clinicalHistory: History of prior arthroscopic stabilization. Recurrent dislocation.
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 36 - Anterior labral re-tear post Bankart repair

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Evidence of anterior labral repair with soft tissue to bone anchors along the anterior glenoid (also on sagittal)
  • Anterior labral tear. Labral tear displaced anteriorly with contrast between the labrum and the underlying glenoid. This shouldn’t be the case post labral repair.
  • Extension of anterior labral tear to the superior labrum. You see a tear cleft that is posterior to the biceps anchor. Overall, labral tearing extends from about 10 to 6 o'clock.
  • Hill Sachs lesion, also see axial images at / above coracoid

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
The labrum stabilizes the shoulder. Tear can lead to instability, pain, loss of function. Labral repair is favored when possible -- re-attaching tissue to improve stability and reduce recurrence.

Normal Post-Repair Labrum is often blunted/rounded, thickened, and heterogeneous with low to intermediate signal from scarring/granulation. But, it should be apposed to glenoid without contrast/fluid dissecting beneath or into labrum.

On routine shoulder MRI after labral repair, fluid-sensitive sequences are essential for highlighting areas of fluid that may track along a tear. However, in the postoperative setting, standard MRI is limited. Given post-repair labral tissue is often thickened, irregular, and heterogeneous on fluid-sensitive sequences, signal abnormality alone should not be equated with recurrent tear unless there is convincing evidence of labral discontinuity combined with fluid tracking at the labral-glenoid interface.

MR arthrography (MRA) has a distinct advantage, making it easier to see labral defects by seeing contrast extending into a defect or between a labral fragment and the glenoid rim -- the hallmark of recurrent detachment. Studies have shown that MRA has high sensitivity (80-90%) and specificity (75-85%) for detecting recurrent glenoid labral tears in postoperative shoulders.

In practice, when the clinical suspicion for recurrent labral tear is high and conventional MRI is equivocal, MRA is the preferred next imaging step because it more confidently distinguishes true labral detachment from expected postoperative changes.

On both standard MRI and MRA, the most reliable sign of a recurrent labral tear is a fluid or contrast-signal cleft extending through or beneath the labral repair, indicating detachment from the glenoid rim. You should look for:

  • A discrete cleft of fluid or contrast between labrum and glenoid.
  • Labral displacement or non-anatomic position relative to the glenoid.
  • Adjacent paralabral cysts that can accompany labral detachment.
  • Associated capsular redundancy or glenohumeral joint effusion.

Compare with prior imaging and operative reports when possible - changes relative to the immediate postoperative baseline can help distinguish healing changes from recurrent pathology.

cases.clinicalHistory: History of prior labral repair. Recent anterior shoulder dislocation playing basketball.

series.label: COR T1 FS post contrast LT
Ax T1 Space FS post contrast

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Evidence of prior labral repair with soft tissue anchors
  • Torn glenoid labrum, extending from posterosuperior, to superior labrum and anterior labrum. Notice the anterior labral tear is displaced with contrast undercutting the labrum.
  • Medialized anteroinferior labral tissue from prior repair
  • Hill Sachs lesion (see axial)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
As mentioned, MRI arthrography is superior to conventional MRI for evaluating the postoperative labrum:

  • Repaired labrum scarring, irregular contour, and altered signal are expected and can obscure recurrent tearing on standard MRI.
  • Intra-articular contrast distends the joint and outlines labral margins, making it easier to identify abnormal contrast extension into or beneath the labrum.

Multiple studies have shown higher sensitivity and specificity of MRI arthrography compared with non-contrast MRI for detecting recurrent labral tears, particularly in the anterior and superior labrum.

MRI Findings:

  • Most reliable MRI arthrography finding of a recurrent tear is contrast extending between the labrum and glenoid or into a labral cleft, particularly when it reaches the base of the labrum.
  • Labral displacement, detachment, or fragmentation supports recurrent tearing.
  • A smooth, blunted labrum without contrast undermining is more consistent with postoperative scarring rather than retear.

A recurrent tear should only be diagnosed when there is definite contrast extension into or under the labrum, labral separation from the glenoid, or abnormal mobility suggested by displaced labral fragments. Simply increased signal or irregular contour without contrast penetration is insufficient.

cases.clinicalHistory: Prior history of labral repair. Moderate to severe shoulder pain.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Evidence of prior labral repair with anchors at the superior and anterior labrum.
  • No contrast cleft undercutting the labral repair sites to suggest a recurrent tear, anteriorly or superiorly.
  • Normal sublabral recess, also on coronal (note its smooth contour, that follows the articular cartilage, towards the glenoid and not away from it). Note it stays anterior to the biceps anchor.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
As mentioned in prior cases, in an intact repair, contrast should not dissect between the labrum and the glenoid.

While standard MRI may show heterogeneous signal or irregular contour that is difficult to interpret, the absence of contrast undermining on MRI arthrography strongly supports labral integrity.

A healed labrum typically appears:

  • Thickened, blunted, and lower in height than a native labrum due to fibrosis.
  • Margins may be irregular
  • Labrum appears firmly apposed to the glenoid without displacement. This water tight configuration is what the surgeon intended. Remember, the role of the labrum is to deepen the socket of the glenoid.

Always review the operative report to understand which portion of the labrum was repaired and how many anchors were used. On MRI arthrography, absence of contrast undermining is the most reliable sign of an intact repair. If the labrum appears irregular but stable with no contrast extension, it is appropriate to confidently report an intact postoperative labrum.

cases.clinicalHistory: History of prior surgery to shoulder
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 39 - Post Latarjet procedure no complications

series.label: AX PD FS DR trial LT
sag T1 dixon DR trial LT_W
sag T1 dixon DR trial LT_F
Cor T2 FS WARP LT rpt

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Evidence of prior Latarjet procedure -- which involves transfer of coracoid process (and attached conjoined tendons of coracobrachialis and short head biceps) to anteroinferior glenoid to treat anterior instability.

  • Note the susceptibility artefacts from the metal screws
  • See 2 screws within the glenoid
  • Blunted coracoid, the donor site for the bone buttress (also on sagittal).
  • Bony buttress from coracoid at this location (obscured by artifact)
  • Mild supraspinatus tendinosis

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
The Latarjet procedure is a surgical stabilization technique used for recurrent anterior shoulder instability, particularly in patients with significant anterior glenoid bone loss or failed prior soft-tissue stabilization. The procedure involves transferring the coracoid process, along with its attached tendons (coracobrachialis, short head biceps), to the anteroinferior glenoid rim.

This works through a combination of mechanisms:

  • Transferred bone restores anterior glenoid bone stock
  • Conjoined tendon creates a dynamic sling that prevents anterior movement of humerus during ABER (abduction and external rotation)
  • Capsule is reinforced by the repair

It's a complex procedure but a solid choice for contact athletes and those with greater glenoid bone loss.

Understanding the anatomy is important because the postoperative anatomy is intentionally altered and should not be mistaken for pathology.

Imaging Appearance:

  • Transferred coracoid graft should be seen along the anteroinferior glenoid rim, typically flush with or slightly medial to the native glenoid articular surface
  • The graft is fixed with one or two screws, which should be intact and appropriately positioned.
  • Over time, there may be partial or complete osseous incorporation between the graft and native glenoid.
  • Here's an Xray post Laterjet

The anterior labrum is often absent or markedly altered, which is expected and should not be interpreted as a recurrent Bankart lesion. The capsule may appear thickened and scarred anteriorly. Mild surrounding marrow edema can be seen in the early postoperative period and gradually resolves.

Which modality?

If osseous bridging of the coracoid bone graft to the anterior glenoid is the clinical question, a CT scan is more appropriate than MRI given artifact. CT is best for assessing the bone / hardware

cases.clinicalHistory: History of renal cell carcinoma with pain
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 40 - Shoulder tumor (metastasis) from renal cell carcinoma

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Expansile lytic lesion within the proximal humerus, seen on both T1 (marrow replacing) and T2 weighted images (hyperintense).
  • Notice the expansion and extension outside of the margins of the humerus on both sagittal and axial planes.
  • Associated edema within infraspinatus, subscapularis and supraspinatus tendons

This was a metastasis from RCC.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
While we will cover bone tumors in a separate course... sometimes, shoulder pain can be due to unexpected causes (i.e. not rotator cuff, cartilage, labrum).We occasionally see bony or soft tissue masses.

On routine shoulder MRI, always evaluate the entire field of view - including the visible bones and soft tissues for lesions.

While these can be incidental, a dedicated tumour protocol MRI should include two orthogonal planes (axial always, and sagittal or coronal based on the location of the lesion) in T1 and T2 fat-suppressed. The entire long bone should be imaged, including the joint above and below.

MRI Findings:

T1-weighted images are critical for detecting marrow replacement, while fluid-sensitive sequences highlight associated edema and soft tissue extension. Typical appearance of lytic metastases:

  • Focal or expansile marrow-replacing lesion, low T1 and high T2
  • Cortical thinning, cortical breakthrough, or an associated soft tissue mass may be present. Look for other aggressive features (ill defined, periosteal reaction, endosteal scalloping, path fracture).
  • Surrounding marrow edema is common and may explain pain in the absence of fracture.
  • Sclerotic mets: T1 low, T2 variable (possible T2 hyperintense halo favors metastasis) - prostate, breast.

In contrast, benign lesions are often:

  • Well defined, narrow zone of transition, no surrounding edema
  • No cortical destruction or soft tissue extension
  • Stability
  • Variable enhancement
  • Enchondromas are very common in proximal humerus (on 2% of routine shoulder MRIs)! See example below.
  • Other proximal humeral lesions you will see include: UBC, ABC, GCT -- but again we will cover these specific entities in a dedicated course, so don't worry about these for now.

Enchondromas appear as low T1, high T2 sharply defined lobulated lesions with internal low signal rings and arcs (characteristic of chondroid matrix). See T1W and T2W of this proximal shoulder enchondroma. Notice the classic low signal ring and arc appearance of chondroid matrix.

cases.clinicalHistory: Mass right shoulder
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 41 - Proximal humerus sarcoma with intra-articular extension

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Intraosseous mass within the proximal humerus, involving the head, neck and proximal diaphysis. See signal on both T1 and T2 weighted images.
  • Intra articular extension of the mass (into the glenohumeral joint)
  • Soft tissue extension anteriorly into deltoid (with cortical defect at that site)
  • Pathological fracture of the humerus through the inferior aspect of the mass (linear low signal)
  • Increased signal with the rotator cuff muscles.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
While tumors are not the focus of this course, we show this case of sarcoma to reinforce a few fundamental concepts.

Protocol:

  • MRI protocol should include the T1W and T2W FS sequences in 2 planes.
  • **Ensure the joint above and below is included in at least one plane, to exclude skip lesions along the long bone

MRI Appearance:

  • Bone sarcoma typically appears as an aggressive marrow-replacing lesion with low T1 signal and heterogeneous high signal on fluid-sensitive sequences
  • Aggressive features, including cortical destruction with extraosseous soft tissue mass
  • Look for intra-articular extension (like in this case -- with tumor breaching the articular cortex and extending directly into the glenohumeral joint). This is often accompanied by enhancing intra-articular soft tissue, synovial infiltration, or tumor nodules within the joint space.
  • Other things to look for / describe: Neurovascular bundle involvement, describe soft tissue extension anatomy including fascial planes involved for surgical planning.

Pearl:

Make sure you always follow the tumor to its margins on all planes and sequences, explicitly assessing whether the articular cortex is violated. When intra-articular extension is present, it should be clearly stated in the report, as this directly affects surgical margins and reconstruction options.
